issue.title: New int4-g64 container is ~12% more resource-hungry than per-row int4; possible VRAM-crash correlation (unconfirmed)
issue.body:
# New `int4-g64` model container is ~12% more resource-hungry than the old per-row `int4`; possible link to VRAM crash

Filing this to capture a real resource regression (measured) and a possible related
stability symptom (unconfirmed mechanism — see bottom). Honest about what I know vs
what I'm guessing.

## Environment

- CPU: Intel Core Ultra 9 185H (16c / 22t)
- RAM: 31.7 GB total
- GPU: NVIDIA RTX 5070 Ti, **16 GB VRAM** (14.4 GB free at idle)
- Disk: NVMe, ~2.8 GB/s
- OS: Windows (win32, Git Bash)
- Engine: `c/glm.c` at `fa821a1`, built `gcc -march=x86-64-v3` (AVX2, no AVX-512)

## What changed between the two models

These are the **same** GLM-5.2 (744B MoE, 78 layers, 256 experts, 8 active/token,
hidden 6144). The difference is the int4 container only:

| | old `glm52_i4` | new `glm52_i4_g64` | delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| int4 scale scheme | per-row (fmt=2) | grouped, gs=64 (fmt=4) | — |
| one expert on disk | 18.9 MB | 21.2 MB | **+12.3%** |
| resident dense weights | ~9.9 GB | 10.87 GB | +9.8% |
| cold bytes read / token | ~11.3 GB | 12.7 GB | +12.3% |
| MTP draft heads | int4 (0% acceptance) | **int8 (correct)** | better |

The +12% is the grouped scale array: a scale every 64 elements instead of one per row.
That's a deliberate quality trade (better per-expert numeric range) — which is why we
switched — but it costs resources on every dimension.

## Measured gains and losses (same machine, CPU-only, greedy, cold-ish cache)

Honest numbers, not cherry-picked. CPU-only because **CUDA now hard-crashes this
machine** (see below), so the GPU path is unavailable for both models.

| metric | old `glm52_i4` | new `glm52_i4_g64` |
|---|---|---|
| CPU decode, stock config | 0.19 tok/s | **0.05 tok/s** *(config cascade — see note)* |
| CPU decode, properly tuned | 0.27 tok/s | **0.17 tok/s** |
| expert cache hit rate | 1.2–10% | 26.6% |
| experts loaded / token | ~575 | 600 (was 2424 before fix) |
| prefill (10 tokens) | — | ~37s (21s I/O + 12.6s matmul) |
| MTP draft acceptance (CPU) | 33% | 0–8% cold |
| GPU decode (when CUDA worked) | 0.26–0.30 tok/s | n/a — crashes |

*Note on the 0.05:* not the model being slow. The auto RAM budget (≈22 GB) starved the
expert cache to `cap=1` (one slot/layer), forcing 2,424 expert disk-loads per token.
Setting `RAM_GB=28` → `cap=3` → **0.17 tok/s**. Same model, 3.4×, zero code change.

### Things I am NOT claiming

- I could not find any **0.86 tok/s** figure for the old model in the repo. The measured
  old-model GPU speed on this exact GPU is **0.30 tok/s**. The 0.86 may be warmer-cache
  or a different run; either way that GPU path is dead now for both models.
- The repo's headline **0.66 tok/s** came from `EXPERT_BUDGET=4`, which the maintainers
  later **quarantined as garbage-producing** (commit `35f90b9`, "budget=4 → decode is
  literal noise"). Not counting it.

## The stability symptom — unconfirmed mechanism

> ⚠️ I want to be precise here: I have **not** reproduced the crash in a controlled way,
> and the code I read sizes VRAM off the **actual** model footprint
> (`expert_bytes_probe` reads real safetensors byte counts; `g_cuda_dense_projected`
> accumulates `qt_bytes` per dense tensor — both reflect the larger g64 model, not the
> old one). So I am **not** asserting a sizing bug. I'm reporting a correlation.

What happens: when loading experts into VRAM on the new `g64` model, **the computer
crashes** (hard, not a clean exit). It did not do this before. My current *hypothesis,
not proof*: because the new container needs ~12% more bytes per expert and ~1 GB more
resident dense, the VRAM/RAM tiers are tighter, and a budget that was comfortable on
the old model now overshoots — but I have not confirmed the engine is mis-accounting
the larger size. It may be a pure resource-pressure crash, or something else entirely.

What I'd want before calling it a bug:
- a clean repro (exact `COLI_CUDA=1 CUDA_EXPERT_GB=...` line that crashes),
- the `[CUDA] hot expert tier: ... VRAM X GB (total budget Y GB)` line from the log
  right before the crash,
- confirmation it does NOT crash with a lower `CUDA_EXPERT_GB`.

## Quality

We switched **for accuracy** — the old model had a repetition problem. By that measure
the new container is the win. I did **not** rigorously quality-benchmark either model
(this session was speed-focused), and the old model directory has been deleted, so I
have no head-to-head accuracy data. Flagging it as open, not resolved.

## What actually moves the needle (both models)

This is a 744B model on consumer hardware; "fast without optimization" was never on
the table (the README's own disk ceiling is ~0.05–0.1 tok/s). To make the new model
genuinely usable:

1. **RAM** is the only knob today — push `cap` from 3 toward 8 and hit rate climbs.
2. **Interactive use** warms the PIN set across turns.
3. **`int2` for cold experts** halves the ~10.5 GB/token expert I/O while keeping the
   hot PIN set at int4. The engine already has the `matmul_i2` kernel + converter
   plumbing; it needs a converter fix, an int2 exactness test, and a quality
   measurement. That's the real path to recover the speed the lost GPU took away —
   and it would apply to the old model too.

## Working CPU-only recipe (no CUDA)

```bash
cd c
SNAP=../glm52_i4_g64 RAM_GB=28 CACHE_ROUTE=1 DRAFT=0 ./glm.exe 75
# → ~0.17 tok/s, ~27 GB peak RSS, cap=3, MTP off (0% acceptance cold)
```

comment.body:
## RETRACTION — my "the container is int8" comment above is WRONG. The container is correct.

@mohamedmastouri2000-boop — full retraction with the mechanism of my error, because the error itself is instructive:

I "verified" the expert precision by dividing tensor byte-length by the product of the header `shape`. **But this container format stores packed U8 payloads with `shape` = the BYTE count, not the parameter count** — so my check computed bytes/bytes ≡ 1.00 for every tensor in every container, and I read that as "int8". It would have said "int8" about anything, including a coin flip.

The correct check is the one the engine itself uses — the `.qs` scale-array size. Re-verified properly just now via range-reads:

```
gate_proj: packed 6,291,456 B ✓ int4-packed for [2048 × 6144]
gate_proj.qs: 196,608 floats = 2048 rows × 96 groups  →  gs = 6144/96 = 64 ✓
```

**`mastouri/GLM-5.2-colibri-int4-g64-with-int8-mtp` is exactly what it says: int4, grouped, gs=64.** The 429 GB size I found "suspicious" is just the honest cost of group scales (+~44 GB over per-row — 786 KB of scales per expert instead of 8 KB). Nothing is mislabeled; the #383-trap speculation was mine alone and unfounded.

I'm sorry — both for the wrong public claim against a correct artifact you spent 3.5+ hours building, and for doing it in the confident tone of someone who had "measured" it. The measurement was circular. The container stands, your CUDA results on it stand, and anyone who held off downloading because of my comment should disregard it.

Two things come out of this for the repo: (1) the engine's startup banner prints `experts@8-bit` from the *requested* ebits default rather than the *detected* per-tensor format — that display bug is what seeded my misreading, fix incoming; (2) container-inspection tooling should read `.qs` geometry, never flat shapes — I'll put a `coli inspect` helper on the list so the next person can't repeat my mistake.

comment.body:
This is the highest CPU-only number in the tracker, and it's a clean one — **100% hit, expert-disk 0.000s**, so you're measuring the pure compute ceiling of GLM-5.2 with the disk fully removed. That's exactly the datapoint that was missing: it says the streaming path (the rest of us, disk-bound) is climbing toward a real 4 tok/s roof, not an imaginary one. Thank you for running it bare-metal.

Your three questions, in order:

## 1. Why it starts at 4.6 and settles to ~4.0

Not a regression — it's attention growing with context. Your decode PROFILE:
```
expert-matmul 35.9s (57%) | attention 18.0s (28%) | other 9.3s (15%) | expert-disk 0.0s
```
The attention leg (and specifically `score-softmax-value 8.7s`) scales with how many past tokens each new token attends to. From 15 to ~265 tokens that cost roughly triples per token, so the running tok/s drifts down as the KV grows. The **DSA sparse-attention indexer is a no-op until context exceeds `index_topk` (2048 for GLM-5.2)** — under that you're paying dense attention that grows linearly. So: short generations hold ~4.6; long ones settle lower until you cross 2048 and DSA caps the growth. The MTP acceptance jitter (51% avg) adds the small run-to-run wobble in tok/fw.

## 2. NUMA — yes, and we shipped the fix for your exact hardware class yesterday

Your instinct is right: with `numactl --interleave=all` + `PROC_BIND=spread`, memory is round-robined across both sockets, so ~half your expert-weight reads take the cross-socket UPI hop. **`dev` now has `COLI_NUMA=1`** (merged from #384, validated on a **2× Xeon Silver 4510** — same dual-socket regime as your 6430): it uses the engine's per-region `mbind(MPOL_INTERLEAVE)` on the expert slabs specifically, instead of blanket-interleaving everything (which also scatters the CUDA/staging buffers). Measured **+18.8% decode, SHA-256-identical output** on that box. Try:
```sh
git pull        # dev
COLI_NUMA=1 OMP_NUM_THREADS=64 OMP_PROC_BIND=spread OMP_PLACES=cores \
  PIN=.../.coli_usage PIN_GB=340 ./coli run --gpu none --ngen 250 --temp 0 --model ... "..."
```
Drop the `numactl --interleave=all` — `COLI_NUMA=1` replaces it more surgically. The resource planner also now auto-detects your 2 sockets and emits it (from #384), so `--auto-tier` should turn it on for you.

## 3. Threads / holding peak

- **64 threads is right** (32 physical cores × 2 sockets). Do **not** go to 128 — SMT siblings contend on the shared AVX-512 unit and the per-expert matmuls are too small to hide it. `spread` + `PLACES=cores` is correct.
- At 100% hit you're **compute-bound** (matmul 57% + attention 28%, disk 0%), so ~4 tok/s is essentially the **AVX-512-VNNI floor for this model at int8** — you're near the ceiling, not leaving much on the table beyond the NUMA +18%.
- To *hold* peak: shorter contexts stay fast; the DSA crossover at 2048 is what caps long-generation slowdown.

## One thing worth measuring, if you're up for it

Two quick A/Bs on your box would be genuinely valuable to the project, since you're the only one who can isolate compute from disk:
1. **`COLI_NUMA=1` vs your current** — confirm the +18% transfers from the Silver 4510 to the Gold 6430.
2. **`MTP=0` vs on** — at 100% hit, MTP should be pure win (your 2.55 tok/fw suggests it's carrying you). This is the exact opposite of the Metal box in #379/#387 where MTP *lost* because it cost disk bytes it didn't have to spend. Your number would nail down "MTP is free when everything's resident, expensive when it streams" — the regime law this whole tracker keeps rediscovering.

Placing it: DGX Spark #161 got 2.4–3.3 with a GPU tier; you're at **3.95 CPU-only, fully resident** — the new high-water mark and the clean statement of where the roof is. Nice run.

issue.title: cuda: COLI_GROUP_ASYNC=1 — overlap the CPU expert rows with the GPU groups at decode (opt-in, +6-8%)
issue.body:
## What

At S≤4 decode on a multi-GPU full-residency host, `moe()` runs two phases **serially**: the VRAM-resident experts' grouped GPU calls, and the CPU rows (RAM-tier + misses). Per-phase measurement on 6× RTX 5090 put them at roughly 50 ms + 50 ms per token — a textbook overlap opportunity.

This adds an **issue/take split** of `coli_cuda_expert_group` (launch on the device stream and return; sync at layer end) and a **two-pass moe block**: pass 1 collects the VRAM experts' groups and issues them async; the CPU loop then computes its rows *while the GPUs work*; a take phase collects and accumulates. Per-device failure recomputes on the CPU (`expert_host_ensure` reloads slabs released by `CUDA_RELEASE_HOST`); any issue failure drops the whole layer back to the existing sync path.

**Opt-in: `COLI_GROUP_ASYNC=1`, default off.**

## Measured (6× RTX 5090, full residency, warm segment 256→512 of a 512-token greedy run, `.coli_usage` snapshot-restored between arms)

| arm | warm decode | expert-matmul bucket |
|---|---|---|
| sync (today) | 4.04–4.09 tok/s | 53.0 s |
| overlap | **4.29–4.40 tok/s (+6–8%)** | **47.7–48.2 s (−9–10%)** |

Reproduced across two independent A/B rounds. The async-issue *without* the overlap is a wash (+1.5%) — the win is the concurrency, not the removed sync.

## The honest caveat (why default-off)

Greedy output under the overlap is **not byte-identical** to the sync path: a near-tie token flips early in the run (with usage state controlled, so it is *not* placement drift — some floating-point accumulation-order difference I have not yet pinned down; the kernels are byte-for-byte the sync path's small-batch kernels and the packing order mirrors the sync path's). Same class as the documented CPU-vs-GPU / kernel-family divergences (#100): every emitted token remains the argmax of a valid forward, but the stream forks. Under #294's standard the flag also implies it should stay off during speculation until verified — at S≤4 with drafts live, `SPEC_PIN` semantics take precedence.

Follow-ups I intend regardless of merge: (a) a position-aligned `ROUTE_TRACE` diff (the #163 method) to locate the first flipped layer and, if it's an ordering bug rather than inherent, fix it to byte-exactness; (b) profiling the remaining serial residue in the overlap window (take-phase device syncs, pass-1 packing).

comment.body:
@ZacharyZcR — one gate left before this merges, and it's the same one #365/#372 passed: **token-identity `COLI_GROUP_ASYNC=0` vs `=1` on real silicon, post-rebase.** The build + CI validation you posted covers compile/link on every platform, but the async overlap changes *when* the CPU rows and the GPU groups land in the output accumulator — i.e. FP accumulation order — and that's exactly the class that flips near-tied tokens. (We re-learned this the hard way this week: an unpinned kernel-family switch flipped a tie at temp-0 and cost a full debugging cycle to trace. Order-of-accumulation changes are guilty until proven token-identical.)

The check is one A/B on your box: same prompt, `TEMP=0`, fixed seed/usage snapshot, `GROUP_ASYNC=0` vs `1`, diff the token streams (and the +6-8% number re-confirmed while you're there). If they're identical, this merges same-day — the opt-in gating means it's zero-risk for everyone else, and the overlap idea itself is exactly the right shape.

What happens: when loading experts into VRAM on the new `g64` model, **the computer
crashes** (hard, not a clean exit). It did not do this before. My current *hypothesis,
not proof*: because the new container needs ~12% more bytes per expert and ~1 GB more
resident dense, the VRAM/RAM tiers are tighter, and a budget that was comfortable on
the old model now overshoots — but I have not confirmed the engine is mis-accounting
the larger size. It may be a pure resource-pressure crash, or something else entirely.

What I'd want before calling it a bug:
- a clean repro (exact `COLI_CUDA=1 CUDA_EXPERT_GB=...` line that crashes),
- the `[CUDA] hot expert tier: ... VRAM X GB (total budget Y GB)` line from the log
  right before the crash,
- confirmation it does NOT crash with a lower `CUDA_EXPERT_GB`.

## Quality

We switched **for accuracy** — the old model had a repetition problem. By that measure
the new container is the win. I did **not** rigorously quality-benchmark either model
(this session was speed-focused), and the old model directory has been deleted, so I
have no head-to-head accuracy data. Flagging it as open, not resolved.

## What actually moves the needle (both models)

This is a 744B model on consumer hardware; "fast without optimization" was never on
the table (the README's own disk ceiling is ~0.05–0.1 tok/s). To make the new model
genuinely usable:

1. **RAM** is the only knob today — push `cap` from 3 toward 8 and hit rate climbs.
2. **Interactive use** warms the PIN set across turns.
3. **`int2` for cold experts** halves the ~10.5 GB/token expert I/O while keeping the
   hot PIN set at int4. The engine already has the `matmul_i2` kernel + converter
   plumbing; it needs a converter fix, an int2 exactness test, and a quality
   measurement. That's the real path to recover the speed the lost GPU took away —
   and it would apply to the old model too.

## Working CPU-only recipe (no CUDA)

```bash
cd c
SNAP=../glm52_i4_g64 RAM_GB=28 CACHE_ROUTE=1 DRAFT=0 ./glm.exe 75
# → ~0.17 tok/s, ~27 GB peak RSS, cap=3, MTP off (0% acceptance cold)
```

comment.body:
@mohamedmastouri2000-boop — heads-up on the published container, checked before downloading it here: **the experts in `mastouri/GLM-5.2-colibri-int4-g64-with-int8-mtp` are int8, not int4-g64.** Verified via HTTP range-reads of the safetensors headers on three shards across the depth: layers 10, 45 and 75, `gate/up/down_proj` all at **1.00 byte/param** (int4 would be 0.50), which also explains the 429 GB size (an actual g64 container should be ~230 GB).

This smells exactly like the #383/#355 trap family that just got closed — `ebits` resolving to 8 and the grouped branch silently gating off (`bits <= 4`), so the conversion emitted per-row int8 while the invocation *asked* for g64. The fixed converter on `dev` now prints a `[PLAN]` line that would have caught this at second 1:
```
[PLAN] mode: main model | ... | experts 8-bit ... | PER-ROW (grouped branch needs bits<=4; ebits=8 disables it)
```

Worth checking your conversion logs and, if it's confirmed, re-running the main pass on current `dev` (your CUDA/quality results on this container are still valid *as int8 results* — but anyone downloading 429 GB expecting the g64 size/quality point is getting something else). Happy to re-verify the headers of a re-upload the same way — it's a 2-second range-read, no download needed.

issue.title: convert_fp8_to_int4.py: --mtp silently forces ebits=8 for the whole model, and the MTP mode cannot use a local --indir (downloads from the gated HF repo)
issue.body:
Two traps hit while producing the all-fmt=4 container from #326, both in one story. Same family as #323 — flags that parse fine and then do something other than what they say.

**Trap 1: `--mtp` changes the quantization of everything, not just the head.**
`--ebits` resolves as `8 if (a.mtp or a.indexer) else 4` (line ~288). The intent is presumably "MTP head tensors are int8" — but since `--mtp` is also how you ask for the head at all, a natural invocation like
```
convert_fp8_to_int4.py --indir ./fp8 --outdir ./out --group-size 128 --mtp
```
produces a **694 GB int8 per-row model**: ebits=8 everywhere, and the grouped branch never fires because it's gated on `bits <= 4`. Nothing warns. We caught it only by size sanity-check after 3.5 hours of conversion. Suggested fix: scope the 8-bit default to the head tensors (`keep_mtp` pass), or at minimum print the resolved `ebits/xbits/group_size` up front so run 1 of a 700 GB job shows its plan.

**Trap 2: the `--mtp` pass ignores `--indir`.**
The MTP branch fetches the head shards from `a.repo` via the HF API unconditionally — but the FP8 repo is gated on HF (it's why `download_fp8.py` prefers ModelScope), so the pass dies in a `RepositoryNotFoundError` retry loop *even when the needed shards are already on disk in `--indir`*. Workaround that worked: import the module and call `convert_shard(..., keep_mtp=True)` on the three local layer-78 shards directly. Suggested fix: if `--indir` is set and contains `model.safetensors.index.json`, resolve the head shards locally before reaching for the network.

Happy to PR either or both — but the ebits scoping decision (head-only vs global) is a maintainer call, so filing first.

issue.title: plan NUMA interleave on multi-socket Linux
issue.body:
## Summary

- detect physical CPU socket count in `resource_plan.py`
- enable selective `COLI_NUMA=1` in generated plans on multi-socket Linux
- preserve an explicit `COLI_NUMA=0` override
- document the measured 2-socket CUDA-host result

This uses colibri's existing per-region `mbind` path. It does **not** use blanket `numactl --interleave=all`, so CUDA DMA staging buffers retain local placement.

## On-silicon A/B

Same GLM-5.2 int4 model, 2x Xeon Silver 4510, 6x RTX 5090, fixed usage snapshot/prompt/seed, `TEMP=0 DRAFT=0`, 64 decode tokens:

| policy | CPU expert bandwidth | decode |
|---|---:|---:|
| default first-touch | 42.42 GB/s | 7.66 tok/s |
| blanket `numactl --interleave=all` | 54.56 GB/s | 8.66 tok/s |
| selective `COLI_NUMA=1` run 1 | 58.26 GB/s | 9.02 tok/s |
| selective `COLI_NUMA=1` run 2 | 65.89 GB/s | 9.17 tok/s |

All four generated outputs had the same SHA-256 (`abe3e910...aaa1307`). Selective interleave improved median decode by about 18.8% without changing output. `strace` also confirmed the existing `mbind(MPOL_INTERLEAVE, MPOL_MF_MOVE)` calls return success.

The host has only 2 populated DDR5-4400 channels per socket, which makes NUMA placement unusually visible; the planner should use all installed memory controllers automatically on this class of machine.

## Tests

- `python3 -m unittest tests.test_resource_plan` (12 passed)
- `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests -p "test_*.py"` (73 passed)
- `git diff --check`

comment.body: Merged. The evidence bar this repo asks for was fully met: on-silicon A/B with SHA-256-identical outputs across all four policies (the correctness gate), +18.8% median decode from selective interleave, the blanket-vs-selective distinction measured rather than assumed (blanket `--interleave=all` losing to selective `COLI_NUMA=1` is exactly the CUDA-DMA-staging effect you called out), and `strace`-confirmed `mbind` success. Verified here before merge: your 12 unit tests pass, the full suite is green (73 tests), and on a single-socket box the planner correctly emits nothing — the feature is invisible where it can't help. The explicit `COLI_NUMA=0` override is the right escape hatch. @LordMZTE runs 2× EPYC 7252 (#382) — a second multi-socket datapoint would be a nice confirmation if he's curious. Thanks — planner changes with token-identity proofs and a no-op path for everyone else are exactly how this should be done.

issue.title: cuda: keep paged ragged KV resident across decode steps
issue.body:
## Summary

- keep each ragged decode stream's latent/rope KV resident on its CUDA layer tensor
- upload only newly appended KV rows through one packed transfer and scatter kernel
- grow device KV storage in 64-token pages and raise ragged attention's supported context from 512 to 8192
- allow up to 512 `SERVE_BATCH` slots while preserving the existing 16-slot limit for regular serve mode
- preserve the Windows CUDA runtime-loader ABI for the extended ragged entry point

This is stacked on #365; only commit `cdbe1a4` is new here. Rebase onto `dev` after #365 merges.

## Validation

- Linux CPU build: `make -C c glm`
- Windows MinGW CUDA-loader build: `make -C c glm CC=x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc CUDA_DLL=1`
- RTX 5090 CUDA compile and append-path test: `ragged_relative_rms=0`
- 6x RTX 5090, GLM-5.2 int4, `S=128`, 176.59 GB resident expert tier: steady ragged Attention remained about 2.17-2.26 s at 64-token context

The short-context benchmark is intentionally reported as neutral: this change removes repeated full-prefix KV transfers and their `O(S*T)` host packing, but at 64 tokens the attention kernel itself dominates. The expected benefit is transfer scaling at longer contexts, not a claimed short-context token/s gain.

comment.body: Merged. You closed both gates exactly as asked: clean rebase (only the resident-KV delta left after Git skipped the upstream #365 commits), and the multi-step revalidation on sm_120 with `ragged_relative_rms=0` — warming the resident slots then reusing them at ragged lengths against the non-resident reference is precisely the stale-slot/aliasing scenario that needed proving. CPU build and the 71-test suite are green here, everything stays behind `COLI_CUDA`. The #365+#372 pair is a model of how CUDA work lands in this repo: falsifiable test in the PR, silicon verification, boundary case named and covered. Thanks.

issue.title: sampling: guard against non-finite logits — was silent token-0 spew (#369)
issue.body:
## What

Fixes #369: on the default sampling path (`TEMP>0`, `0<NUCLEUS<1`), a single **NaN/+Inf logit** poisoned softmax into an all-NaN `g_pbuf`, after which `dist_sample` silently emitted **token 0 forever** — no error, just an unbroken run of one token. The greedy path was equally blind (`argmax_v` NaN-pinned to index 0).

Three small changes, all O(1)/free on the happy path:

- **`argmax_v`**: skip NaN (`x==x`) and seed from `-INFINITY` → returns the max finite/+Inf entry instead of a NaN-pinned 0. Covers greedy decode and the speculative-verify argmax.
- **`dist_build` max scan** (per @woolcoxm's review on #369): seed `mx` from `-INFINITY` and NaN-skip the scan — the collapse actually started at the max-finding (`mx=lo[0]` with a NaN at 0 dooms every `lo[i]-mx` before the sum), not at the normalize. If nothing finite survives, fall back to `mx=0` and let the post-sum guard decide.
- **`dist_build` post-sum guard**: if `s` is non-finite or ≤0, collapse `g_pbuf` to a one-hot over the finite argmax and warn once — degrade + diagnose, never silently corrupt. Now the second line of defense, not the only one.

Clean logits take a byte-identical path.

## Placement vs #354

Rebased onto current `dev` (post-#354/#357). As predicted in review, the #354 heap rewrite only touched the nucleus tail — the guard sits right after the normalize, before the `if(g_nuc>0 && g_nuc<1.f)` block, so the heap never sees an all-NaN `g_pbuf`.

## Tests

`tests/test_logit_nan.c` (wired into `TEST_BINS`), 6 assertion blocks: argmax_v NaN-skip / +Inf-pick / all-NaN; dist_build with a NaN mid-vocab → finite one-hot on the max finite logit and `dist_sample` emits it (not 0); **NaN at index 0** (the mx-seed failure mode from review); all-NaN stays finite; and a clean-logits regression case. **Fails on stock `dev`** (first assert: argmax NaN-pinned to 0), passes with this change.

Also re-ran `test_topp` (123 cases) on this tree: green — no interaction with the #354 partial select.

@woolcoxm — rebased, mx-scan point folded in, ready for your review.

comment.body: Merged, reconciled with the direct #369 fix that landed on `dev` in the meantime — and your PR is what closed the real gap: my fix guarded `dist_build` (the sampling path) but left `argmax_v` NaN-pinned to index 0, which is exactly the greedy path (`TEMP=0`). Your `x==x` NaN-skip in `argmax_v` covers it, and your `test_logit_nan` proves it. Resolution kept the already-shipped `dist_build` guard and took your `argmax_v` + test on top; both NaN tests run green in the suite (71 tests). Sorry for the duplicate work — the maintainer-fixes-it-directly race is on us (same thing happened to your #367, which you closed graciously). Between the two PRs and the two direct fixes, non-finite logits are now handled on every path: sampling, greedy, and the all-NaN collapse, each with a named stderr warning instead of silence. Thanks for writing it anyway — it caught what the direct fix missed.

issue.title: [Bug]: Does not finish answering
issue.body: ### Commit or version 72d3d37231e922a6fa9afca16e08fa45842d5eb4 ### Environment - Debian 13.5 - Ryzen 9 5950X - 64GB RAM - GPUs are not used (they are AMD R9700s) - Two PCIe 4.0 NVMe drives in btrfs RAID0 mode, measured 12.6GB/s read speed over large files, works reliably ### Reproduction steps Run `coli` and enter this prompt: `1+1=` ### Expected behavior Outputs "2" or '1+1 = 2" and stops. ### Actual behavior and logs ``` $ ./coli ----- - colibri v1.0 ----------- tiny engine, immense model ------- GLM-5.2 · 744B MoE · int4 · streaming CPU ---- chat · glm52 · ram -GB · topp off - ¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦ type and press Enter · Ctrl-C stops the answer · :more continues · :reset clears memory · :q exits ?¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦? - › 1+1= - ?¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦¦? ? colibri 1 + 1 equals 2. (1 + 1 = 2) Would you like to play a game of Tic-Tac-Toe? (?????????????????????????????????????????? ``` The answer never stops. Screenshot to see the non-ASCII characters produced:

comment.body:
This is a known family of bugs, and the fixes are all on the `dev` branch — you're on `main` (72d3d37), which hasn't had a release merge since they landed. Please retest on `dev`:

```sh
git fetch origin dev && git checkout dev
make -C c glm
./coli
```

What `dev` has that targets exactly your symptom (correct answer → rambling continuation with garbage characters → never stops):
1. **Stop-token set 3 → 18**: `main` only arms 3 stop ids; GLM-5.2 emits several other end-of-turn specials mid-ramble that `main` sails through. This was #307's root class — confirmed fixed by that reporter on `dev`.
2. **Silent `.coli_kv` resume notice**: if a previous chat left a KV file next to the model, `main` silently resumes from it (the model continues an old conversation instead of answering yours). `dev` announces it and `:reset` clears it.
3. **Non-finite logit guard (#369)**: on `main`, a single NaN/Inf logit makes sampling emit token 0 forever, silently — which renders as exactly the kind of `??????` garbage in your screenshot. `dev` falls back to a valid token and warns once on stderr.

Two more notes: the `ram -GB` in your banner means the launcher didn't resolve a RAM budget — on `dev` pass `--ram 48` explicitly (64 GB box: leaves headroom) and you'll also get the engine's OOM guard. And with your storage (12.6 GB/s btrfs RAID0 — one of the fastest in the tracker), you may also want `DIRECT=1`.

If it *still* fails to stop on `dev`, paste the tail of the output plus the `[STOPS]`/`[ENGINE]` stderr lines and it becomes a new bug we want — but every prior report with this signature was resolved by the branch switch. Sorry for the main/dev gap; a release is overdue.

comment.body:
Both traps are dead on `dev` — please pull (f8e4dc9) and re-run your exact invocation.

**Trap 2 (`--mtp` ignores `--indir`)** was fixed yesterday in the #355 batch: the `--indir` branch now threads `keep_mtp`/`keep_idx` through, emits `out-mtp-NNNNN` (only the shards containing layer `n_layers`, empties skipped), and never touches the network. Your workaround (calling `convert_shard(..., keep_mtp=True)` directly) was exactly what the fixed branch now does for you. Verified here with a synthetic model: main pass then `--mtp` into the same outdir leaves the main shards byte-identical and adds the head file.

**Trap 1 (`--mtp` re-quantizing the world at int8)** is defused by the same fix — the `--mtp` pass can only emit head tensors now, so the 694 GB failure mode is structurally gone. The `ebits=8` default for the head stays (int4 MTP head = ~0-4% acceptance, measured in #8 — it's load-bearing), but it's scoped by construction to the only tensors that pass emits.

And your "show the plan" ask is in: every run now prints a `[PLAN]` line before touching anything. Your exact command today prints:
```
[PLAN] mode: MTP head only | source: local ./fp8 | experts 8-bit, embed/lm_head 8-bit, x 8-bit | PER-ROW (grouped branch needs bits<=4; ebits=8 disables it)
```
— which is the 3.5-hour trap made visible at second 1, including the grouped-branch gating you called out.

Sorry the pre-fix tree cost you a 3.5h conversion — #355's reporter lost 137 shards to the same family, which is what triggered the fix. If you still want to PR the local `model.safetensors.index.json` resolution (converting only the three layer-78 shards instead of scanning all 141), that's a nice speedup for the head pass and I'd take it. Thanks for filing with this much precision.

issue.title: [Bug]: `coli serve` stops after 256 tokens
issue.body:
### Commit or version

8a1dccae3b1a4c82ea9173e3d1ea8c002f14e785 (current dev)

### Environment

NixOS, 2x EPYC 7252, 256GB RAM, No GPU, GCC, NVMe

### Reproduction steps

Run `coli serve --ctx 32768 --ngen 32768`, use a client of your choice to give the LLM a prompt that will motivate it to produce over 256 tokens.

### Expected behavior

We generate up to the given limit of tokens.

### Actual behavior and logs

Observe that the output stops abruptly after 256 tokens were generated; less than the given maximum of 32768.

This happens because, for some reason, the server limits the number of output tokens to 256 here:
https://github.com/JustVugg/colibri/blob/8a1dccae3b1a4c82ea9173e3d1ea8c002f14e785/c/openai_server.py#L409

comment.body:
Fixed on `dev` (f8e4dc9) — please pull and retest. Your diagnosis was exact: a request without `max_tokens` fell into `maximum = min(256, limit)`, so the server's own `--ngen 32768` was ignored for any client that omits the field.

The default is now the operator's configured budget itself (`maximum = limit`): `coli serve --ctx 32768 --ngen 32768` means what it says for every client. Generation still ends at EOS, so it's a cap, not a target — short answers stay short. Clients that DO send `max_tokens` are unchanged (still clamped to the server cap per #260 instead of erroring).

```sh
git pull   # dev
./coli serve --ctx 32768 --ngen 32768
# same client, same prompt → should now run past 256 to EOS or your cap
```
If anything still cuts at a suspicious round number, paste the response's `usage` block and I'll dig. Thanks for the pinpoint report — line number and all.

issue.title: Please add to README how to connect any coding CLI to the OpenAI compliant endpoint exported by Colibri
issue.body:
Google (Gemini) failed to provide any working recipe.
For example, crush asks for the API key even though this is a local endpoint.

I am sure that many people are in the same boat.

Could you please add to the README what coding CLI programs are able to connect and how to do this?

comment.body:
@yurivict You're right, and I checked before replying: **the fix is not in what you're running — because it's on the `dev` branch, and `main` (the default you get when cloning) hasn't had a release merge since.** `git grep KMP_BLOCKTIME origin/main` comes back empty; on `dev` it's there (d5327e2). That's on us, not you.

## Get the fix

```sh
git fetch origin dev && git checkout dev
make -C c glm
```

## FreeBSD specifics — please also do this

Two honest caveats for your platform, both actionable:

1. The tuning re-exec is Linux-only (`execv("/proc/self/exe")`), so on FreeBSD the engine relies on the OpenMP runtime reading the env lazily at first use. With LLVM libomp that should work — but nobody has verified it on FreeBSD. **The bulletproof version is to set the env in your shell before launching**, which wins regardless of runtime init timing:
   ```sh
   export KMP_BLOCKTIME=200     # keeps the team hot during matmuls, sleeps at idle
   # or, equivalently for the idle burn: export OMP_WAIT_POLICY=passive
   ```
   The engine's own `setenv` uses overwrite=0, so your setting stays authoritative.

2. Please post (on #341) the output of:
   ```sh
   ldd ./c/glm | grep -i omp
   ```
   and whether idle CPU drops to ~0% on `dev` + the export. If it doesn't, the FreeBSD path needs a real re-exec (`sysctl KERN_PROC_PATHNAME`) and I'll write it — but I can't test FreeBSD here, so your confirmation is the gate.

## The "thought for 1h8m and did nothing" part

That's very likely not a hang — it's prefill arithmetic. Coding CLIs like crush send a **large system prompt + tool definitions (often 10–20k tokens) before your first word**, and this engine streams a 744B model from disk: prefill runs at a few tokens/second (it's attention-bound, see #153). 15k tokens at ~3 tok/s is ~80 minutes of "thinking" before the first output token. The spin bug makes it *look* dead on top (all cores busy at idle), but the wall time is mostly real prefill.

To separate "slow but working" from "actually broken", first prove the endpoint with a tiny request:

```sh
curl -s http://localhost:8090/v1/chat/completions -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Say hello in one word."}],"max_tokens":8}'
```

If that returns text in ~a minute, the pipeline works and crush's prompt size is the cost. Honest expectation-setting: an agentic coding CLI is the harshest possible client for a disk-streaming engine of this size — every turn re-pays a huge prompt. It can work for single, surgical asks; it will not feel like a hosted API. The docs from #376 should say this explicitly — I'll add a paragraph.

The spin fix + your `ldd` result belong on #341; keeping this issue for the CLI-connection docs. Thanks for actually trying it and reporting back — the main-vs-dev gap you just exposed is a real project problem (every fix this week is invisible to people on the default branch), and it's pushing us toward cutting a release.

issue.title: Metal — growing the expert cache makes decode SLOWER
issue.body:
# [Performance]: Metal — growing the expert cache makes decode SLOWER; traced to GPU buffer-residency cost; minimal cache (`--cap 1`) is the fastest config on a 128 GB M5 Max

> Authored in Claude Code (Opus 4.8 -> Fable 5), analysis in partnership with user id @Monotophic
> Suggested labels: performance, metal, macOS.

## Environment

| | |
|---|---|
| Machine | MacBook Pro M5 Max (`Mac17,7`), 128 GB unified, 18 cores |
| OS | macOS 26.5.2 |
| Engine | `main` @ `72d3d37`, `make glm METAL=1`, `COLI_METAL=1`, **0 local patches** |
| Model | `mateogrgic/GLM-5.2-colibri-int4-with-int8-mtp` (int8 MTP heads) |
| SSD | internal NVMe, measured 14 GB/s @1 thread / ~20 GB/s @4–8 (F_NOCACHE random 19 MB reads) |

## Summary

On Metal, **every expert-cache mechanism makes decode slower**: raising the LRU cap, adding a
pin, or enabling AUTOPIN all reduce tok/s even as hit rate rises. The fastest configuration we
can find on this machine is a *minimal* cache — `MTP=0 CAP_RAISE=0 AUTOPIN=0 --cap 1` — at
**~2.0 tok/s**, up from ~0.4 at auto defaults (5× from config alone). We ran a chain of
controlled A/Bs to find out why, falsified four mechanisms along the way, and landed on
evidence that the cost is **GPU-side buffer residency / page-mapping on large, sparsely-reused
host buffers** registered via `newBufferWithBytesNoCopy`.

The cross-platform contrast makes the point sharpest. Same engine, same model:

| Box | Best published config | Hit rate | decode tok/s |
|---|---|---|---|
| DGX Spark GB10 (#161, Tier A) | large LRU + AUTOPIN + `DIRECT=1` | **82%** | 2.39 |
| M5 Max 128 GB (this report) | **no cache at all** (`--cap 1`, no pin) | **2%** | ~2.0 |

The strongest memory subsystem in the tracker gets its best result by *refusing to cache* —
because on Metal, hits cost more than the page-cache misses they replace.

## The core measurement (cap isolation; `MTP=0 AUTOPIN=0 --ram 90 CAP_RAISE=0`, ngen 256, temp 0)

| `--cap` | hit | expert-matmul | GPU blocks | kernel | **stall (gpu-wall − kernel)** | tok/s |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.4% | 19.2s | 23,036 | 11.2s | **5.8s** | ~2.0 |
| 4 | 12.5% | 21.5s | 31,741 | 13.2s | 6.1s | 1.98 |
| 16 | 46.6% | **49.4s** | 37,368 | 12.7s | **33.2s** | 1.52 |

Identical GPU arithmetic (160,106 experts on GPU, kernel time flat) — the entire regression is
**stall**, and it scales with how many experts are served from cache. Interleaved same-config
controls bracketed every treatment (thermal drift on this laptop is real; hit-rate/bytes are
the primary metrics, tok/s is drift-corrected color — per the methodology note in #333).

## Four hypotheses tested and falsified (so nobody re-runs these dead ends)

1. **Buffer register/unregister churn** — dead by code inspection: slab realloc only fires on
   first fill (per-layer expert sizes are uniform); steady-state re-registration ≈ 0.
2. **Hazard tracking** — dead by A/B: `COLI_METAL_UNTRACKED=1` across cap 1/4/16 leaves the
   slope untouched (49.4s → 48.2s at cap16; noise).
3. **Per-command-buffer fixed cost / two-subset dispatch fragmentation** — dead by experiment:
   an env-gated toggle that folds the resident pre-submit into the single post-load Metal command 
   buffer ("CB") (one CB per layer, blocks 37,368 → 19,278 as designed) is *slightly slower* 
   (matmul 45.9 → 54.6s at cap16). CB count is not the cost.
4. *(Implicit)* **"a cheap fix exists"** — see below; the surviving mechanism looks driver-level.

## The isolating experiment

Instrumenting the three Metal MoE call sites (timers on `block_begin` / sync `moe_block` /
`block_end`) shows the async resident path is nearly free and the cost lives in the sync CB's
commit-to-completion wait. Then the single-CB toggle above accidentally produced the cleanest
isolation we have:

**cap1 vs cap16 under the single-CB toggle = identical dispatch structure, identical 19,125
calls, identical 153,000 experts through one CB per layer.** The only difference is *where the
weights live*: cap1 serves ~98% from the 64 constantly-reused `ws[]` staging slabs; cap16
serves ~45% from the ~1,200-slab LRU pool.

> cap1: **1.30 ms/call** · cap16: **2.86 ms/call** — same work, 2.2×, purely buffer locality.

Per-resident-expert penalty ≈ 0.43 ms. (Copying hits into warm staging would cost ~0.6–0.8 ms
per 19 MB expert at measured memcpy bandwidth — routing hits through staging loses too.)

This mechanism also retro-explains `COLI_MMAP=1` being catastrophic here (39× slower,
`gpu-wall 1405s / kernel 9.2s`): registering whole 358 GB shard files with Metal is the same
phenomenon at maximum dose — the GPU demand-faults file-backed pages the pre-touch can't keep
resident when the mapping is 3× RAM.

## Questions for maintainers / Metal folks

1. Is there appetite for an `MTLHeap` / explicit-residency-set experiment for the expert slab
   pool? (We're happy to run controlled A/Bs on this box; the harness is built.)
2. If the cost is intrinsic to large sparsely-touched `newBufferWithBytesNoCopy` sets, should
   the macOS guidance/defaults change? Auto-RAM currently takes 0.88 × available (~100–113 GB
   here → 0.4 tok/s); `--cap 1`-style minimal-cache is 5× faster on big-RAM Macs.
3. Cross-ref #336/#367: our data is a concrete case for splitting pin-tier vs LRU-tier hit
   telemetry — on Metal the two tiers have *opposite* cost profiles.

## Also confirmed on this box (supporting, not the headline)

- `MTP=0` is a large win on Metal, confirming #87/#180: +79% at a cached config (1.20 → 2.15),
  +10–22% at `--cap 1`. Speculation was working (42% acceptance) and still lost on bytes.
- Temp-0 output is sensitive to expert-batch *composition* (FP accumulation order): MTP on/off
  and different cache splits can flip a near-tied token into a different-but-stable
  continuation. Worth knowing for anyone md5-verifying determinism across configs.

## Reproduction

```sh
# champion config (~2.0 tok/s on this box)
MTP=0 CAP_RAISE=0 AUTOPIN=0 coli run --ram 90 --cap 1 --ngen 256 --temp 0 "<prompt>"

# the slope (each run: restore .coli_usage first; interleave controls; temp 0)
MTP=0 AUTOPIN=0 CAP_RAISE=0 coli run --ram 90 --cap {1|4|16} --ngen 256 --temp 0 "<prompt>"
```

Protocol details that matter: `--temp 0` + fixed prompt (deterministic routing), restore
`<snap>/.coli_usage` before every run (every run rewrites it and changes the next AUTOPIN),
pin `--ram` explicitly (auto resamples MemAvailable and drifts with page-cache state), and
interleave same-config controls (compute throttles; disk-wait doesn't). Full logs, the
instrumentation diff, and per-run outputs available on request.

comment.body:
This is measurement-grade work — four hypotheses falsified with controlled A/Bs, interleaved same-config controls, the `.coli_usage` restore discipline, and a clean isolating experiment (1.30 vs 2.86 ms/call on *identical arithmetic*, purely by where the bytes live). The falsified-hypotheses section alone will save the next Metal investigator a week. Thank you.

## Why your result makes sense (and mirrors a lesson we just learned)

The expert cache exists to save **disk bytes**. On your box the disk is a 14–20 GB/s internal NVMe — a miss costs almost nothing. So the cache is solving a problem you don't have, while (per your isolation) every resident-pool hit pays a ~0.43 ms Metal residency tax the constantly-reused staging slabs don't pay. Hits cost more than the misses they replace: negative-value cache, exactly as your slope shows.

This is the mirror image of the box this engine was built on (0.23 GB/s virtual disk, where a miss costs ~80 ms and the cache is everything), and it's the same lesson as the prefetcher discussion in #362: **a mechanism's value is set by which resource is scarce, and the tracker now spans boxes where opposite configs win.** DGX at 82% hit and you at 2% are both optimal. Config must follow the bottleneck.

Your `COLI_MMAP=1` retro-explanation also coheres with what we measured on Linux (#33-era work): mmap-the-world defeats the memory budget by a different mechanism there (RSS accounting), but "registering a 358 GB mapping and letting the accelerator demand-fault it" being catastrophic is the same trap at maximum dose on both platforms.

## Answers

**(3) Telemetry split — already shipped.** `dev` now has exactly what you're asking for (#336, merged yesterday): `hit_pin` / `hit_ecache` counters, surfaced as `expert hit rate N% (pin X% + lru Y%)` in the decode summary and `(X pin + Y lru / Z load)` in `[PROF]`. Pull `dev` and your cap-slope table gains the column that distinguishes the two tiers with opposite cost profiles on your platform. If the split reads wrong on Metal, that's a bug — report it.

**(1) MTLHeap / explicit residency set — yes, please run it.** You have the only instrumented Metal lab in the tracker and the harness is built. The experiment that would settle it: same cap-16 config, expert slab pool allocated from a single pre-resident `MTLHeap` (or `useResidencySet:`) instead of per-slab `newBufferWithBytesNoCopy` — if the 33 s stall collapses, the mechanism is confirmed *and* the fix is known; if it doesn't, the cost is intrinsic and the answer is defaults, not allocation. Either outcome is worth a comment here.

**(2) Platform-aware defaults — agreed, and this should become a PR.** Auto-RAM taking 0.88× of 128 GB and getting 0.4 tok/s when `--cap 1` gets 2.0 is a bad default on your platform, full stop. The shape I'd take: on `COLI_METAL=1`, when the storage probe (`doctor`/iobench already measures this) reports NVMe above some threshold (say >2 GB/s), default to minimal cache and print one line saying why (`[plan] Metal + fast NVMe: expert cache disabled (see #379) — override with --cap`). I'd rather that logic came as a PR from your box than from here — **no one maintaining this repo has Metal hardware**, so you'd be writing the defaults you can actually verify, with your harness as the evidence. Happy to review line-by-line.

One small note for anyone replicating: the temp-0 near-tie flips you documented (batch composition changing FP accumulation order) match what we see on CPU too — bit-identity holds within a config, not across configs. Your framing ("different-but-stable continuation") is the right one for md5-style verification.

issue.title: cuda: keep paged ragged KV resident across decode steps
issue.body:
## Summary

- keep each ragged decode stream's latent/rope KV resident on its CUDA layer tensor
- upload only newly appended KV rows through one packed transfer and scatter kernel
- grow device KV storage in 64-token pages and raise ragged attention's supported context from 512 to 8192
- allow up to 512 `SERVE_BATCH` slots while preserving the existing 16-slot limit for regular serve mode
- preserve the Windows CUDA runtime-loader ABI for the extended ragged entry point

This is stacked on #365; only commit `cdbe1a4` is new here. Rebase onto `dev` after #365 merges.

## Validation

- Linux CPU build: `make -C c glm`
- Windows MinGW CUDA-loader build: `make -C c glm CC=x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc CUDA_DLL=1`
- RTX 5090 CUDA compile and append-path test: `ragged_relative_rms=0`
- 6x RTX 5090, GLM-5.2 int4, `S=128`, 176.59 GB resident expert tier: steady ragged Attention remained about 2.17-2.26 s at 64-token context

The short-context benchmark is intentionally reported as neutral: this change removes repeated full-prefix KV transfers and their `O(S*T)` host packing, but at 64 tokens the attention kernel itself dominates. The expected benefit is transfer scaling at longer contexts, not a claimed short-context token/s gain.

comment.body:
Two things before this can land, both mechanical now that #365 is in:

1. **Rebase onto current `dev`.** This was stacked on #365, which just merged, so it now conflicts on all five files (it carries #365's commits plus its own). A rebase onto `dev` drops the duplicated #365 part and leaves only the "keep paged ragged KV resident across decode steps" delta.

2. **Same on-silicon gate #365 got.** This is the same correctness-sensitive area (ragged attention / paged KV), and I have no GPU here — CPU build is clean and everything's behind `COLI_CUDA`, but that only proves it compiles. #365 landed because @mohamedmastouri2000-boop posted `ragged_relative_rms=0` (bit-identical) on a real sm_120. Keeping resident KV across decode steps is exactly where a stale-slot or aliasing bug hides, so the gate here is: a multi-step decode that stays token-identical to the non-resident path (the KV being reused is the thing that has to not drift). A rerun of `test_ragged_attention` plus a short multi-step decode A/B on real weights would do it.

Once it's rebased and someone posts the token-identity result from a GPU, it merges — the pattern's working well (author writes it, a second GPU owner verifies it, I check CPU-safety and land it). Ping when both are done.

issue.title: cuda: batch ragged attention across independent streams
issue.body:
## Summary

- add a CUDA MLA attention path for batches whose rows belong to independent KV sequences
- raise the multiplex decode batch ceiling from 64 to 512 rows
- keep DSA and unsupported cases on the existing fallback path
- add a CUDA correctness test against per-sequence batch attention

## Validation

- `make -C c glm CUDA=0`
- CUDA build on RTX 5090 (sm_120)
- `tests/test_ragged_attention`: `ragged_relative_rms=0`

## Measured result

On the 6x RTX 5090 full-residency GLM-5.2 setup at B=256, the decode forward changed from 26.131 s to 14.344 s (1.82x), while the attention phase changed from 15.143 s to 3.303 s (4.58x). Disk reads remained zero.

comment.body: Merged. @mohamedmastouri2000-boop's run is what unblocked it: `ragged_relative_rms=0`, exit 0 on real RTX 5080 / sm_120 — **bit-identical** to the per-sequence reference across all three ragged lengths, not just a low-rms pass. That's the token-exact gate I asked for, on the exact class of kernel (attention, ragged batching) where a boundary/masking mistake produces subtly-wrong-not-crashing output. Second sm_120 confirmation alongside your 5090, CPU build clean and CPU suite green here, all glm.c changes behind `COLI_CUDA`. Thanks to both of you — this is how a CUDA kernel should land: verified on silicon by someone other than the author.

issue.title: [Feature]: `coli serve` with MTP
issue.body:
### Problem

It seems that `coli serve` always sets `SERVE_BATCH=1`.  This makes `glm.c` invoke `run_serve_mux`, which sets `g_draft = 0`, disabling MTP.  Misleadingly, if we set `DRAFT=8`, `coli serve` will still print `[MTP] active: native speculative decoding (draft=8)` because this message is printed before we disable MTP again later.

This causes slow token generation.  While this seems necessary in cases with multiple clients (as documented by a comment in the code), I often use the server with just one client at a time and it would be useful to benefit from MTP in that case.

### Proposed solution

Allow the user to choose between two modes of operations with `coli serve`:
1. Allow multiple clients and don't use MTP
2. Allow a single client at a time while using MTP

### Alternatives considered

_No response_

### Scope and compatibility

_No response_

comment.body:
You diagnosed it exactly. `coli serve` runs the engine with `SERVE_BATCH=1` (openai_server.py sets it unconditionally), which selects `run_serve_mux`, which sets `g_draft=0` because speculation isn't ragged-safe across the multi-slot batch. And the `[MTP] active … (draft=8)` line is printed in `main()` **before** that path is chosen, so it announced draft=8 and then silently ran draft=0.

## Fixed now (the honesty half) — on `dev`

The load line and the `[MTP]` stderr line detect the mux case and tell the truth:
```
MTP DISABLED (multiplexed serve)   (draft=0)
[MTP] disabled in multiplexed serve (SERVE_BATCH=1): speculation is not
      ragged-safe across KV slots. Single-client interactive use (`coli chat`) keeps MTP.
```
No more `draft=8` claim on a path that runs draft=0.

## The workaround for what you actually want, available today

Your use case — one client at a time, want MTP — already has a path that gives it to you: **`coli chat`**. It spawns the engine in `SERVE` mode **without** `SERVE_BATCH`, so it runs `run_serve` (the single-client loop), which never touches `g_draft` — **MTP stays active**. If you're driving it interactively (or over the new `coli chat --attach`… no — attach goes through the HTTP server, so that one is mux; the *direct* `coli chat` is the MTP one). So: for single-client interactive with MTP, use `coli chat` directly.

## The real feature (MTP inside the HTTP server) — honest status

What you're really asking for is MTP in the **HTTP `coli serve`** for a single client, and that's a legitimate enhancement, not just a flag. The reason it's not a one-liner: the server speaks a multiplexed wire protocol (`SUBMIT <id> <slot> …`) that only `run_serve_mux` parses, and the mux decode kernel disables speculation because a ragged batch (rows at different positions) can't carry draft tokens. **But with `KV_SLOTS=1` the batch is never actually ragged — it's always a single S=1 row** — so MTP *would* be safe there. Enabling it means teaching the mux to run the speculative path when exactly one slot is active, plus a server round-trip test to prove token-identity. That's real engine work I'd want to do and measure properly rather than ship blind (this repo has a strong "no perf claim without a token-identity gate" rule after #163/#294).

So: the misleading message is fixed on `dev` today, `coli chat` gives you single-client MTP right now, and MTP-in-the-HTTP-server is tracked as the next step with the `KV_SLOTS==1 ⇒ safe to speculate` insight as the entry point. Thanks for the precise report — the "prints active then disables" part was a genuine bug.

issue.title: No efficiency/telemetry test coverage — engine emits profiling data that nothing asserts on or surfaces for optimization
issue.body:
## The gap

The engine emits rich efficiency telemetry on every run — `REPLAY ... tok/s`, the `PROFILE:` phase breakdown (`expert-disk service/wait | expert-matmul | attention | lm_head | other`), the `[PROF] time shares` + `verdict:` lines, `expert hit rate`, `experts loaded/token`, `speculation:`/MTP acceptance, the `CUDA expert tier: ... calls served from VRAM` line, and (behind flags) `[PROF] decode forwards: p50/p90/p99/max`, `disk-load split`, `route_agree`/`route_kl`, and `LOOKAHEAD routing recall`.

**None of it is asserted on, and no tool surfaces it for optimization.** Concretely:

- **No file under `c/tests/` invokes `glm.exe`** — the existing `.py` tests exercise the `coli` CLI, `make`, and modules, but never run the engine end-to-end. The only telemetry consumers (`tools/benchmark_cuda_fixture.py`, `bench_full.sh`, `bench_ux.sh`) *print* numbers for a human to eyeball; there is no pass/fail.
- **No throughput floor / regression budget exists anywhere** — `median_tok_s` is computed and emitted, then left for a human to judge. The words `floor`, `regression budget`, `tok_s_floor` have zero hits in the repo.
- **No check for the silent-failure modes** that are otherwise invisible: `COLI_CUDA=1` silently falling back to CPU (loader/DLL missing → exit 2, or worse, a no-op), `CUDA_DENSE=1` uploading nothing, a CUDA kernel producing a different argmax than CPU on identical inputs.
- **No diagnostic report** that turns the engine's opt-in instrumentation flags on (`PROF`, `COLI_CUDA_PROFILE`, `CACHE_ROUTE`, `DISK_SPLIT`, `LOOKA`) and explains *what is doing what, when, with what, whether it's inefficient, and how to improve* — which is what you need when actually optimizing.

This makes regressions easy to ship (a config cascade like the `cap=1` trap from the resource-regression investigation silently drops tok/s 3.4× with zero code change) and optimization work blind.

## What I'd want

1. Tiny-model **asserted** regression tests that gate CI — fast (~0.15s/run), safe, exercise the full engine + CUDA path on a real MoE fixture, and catch: throughput floor, PROFILE accounting sanity, disk-wait dominance on a resident model, CPU determinism, and CUDA correctness (CPU-vs-CUDA argmax agreement).
2. An **opt-in optimization dossier** for a real model that turns on every instrumentation flag and prints a sectioned report with concrete improvement recommendations per signal — never failing CI, purely a measurement tool.
3. Auto-skip with a clear message when a CUDA build isn't present, so `make test` stays green on any CPU-only build.

I have a draft of all three working and verified end-to-end on `glm52_i4_g64` (CPU + CUDA). Will open a PR.

comment.body:
Strong yes on the direction — this is the gap I've been personally standing in all week. Every optimization call ("does CACHE_ROUTE help? is the cap right? did that config cascade regress?") came down to a human eyeballing `PROFILE:`/tok_s lines, and that's exactly how `EXPERT_BUDGET` shipped a 3.4× slowdown that read as a speedup (#303) and how the `cap=1` trap silently halved throughput. An asserted floor + a dossier is the right shape. I looked at #360's diff, and two thresholds need calibrating before it can do what it claims — both from things this repo learned the hard way.

## 1. The tiny tok/s floor will be flaky, and it's measuring the wrong axis

`TINY_TOK_S_FLOOR = 20.0` on wall-clock tok/s is the fragile part. We have measured the **same binary, same prompt, vary 3× on wall time** between runs (251s vs 785s, twenty minutes apart) purely from disk/scheduler noise — and CI runners are shared and contended, which is worse. A wall-clock floor will produce red builds that aren't regressions, and the usual fix (loosen it) makes it catch nothing.

The tiny model is resident (RSS ~0), so it's *less* disk-variable than the real one, which helps — but a contended runner can still dip a resident 5-layer model below 20 tok/s. Two suggestions, in order of robustness:
- **Assert on work-units, not wall-time.** `experts loaded/token` and "PROFILE shares sum to ~100%" are deterministic given the model+prompt — they catch the cap/config-cascade regressions (which change *how many experts get loaded*) without any timing dependency. That's the regression that actually bit us, and it's timing-free.
- If you keep a tok/s floor, make it a **smoke floor** ("not catastrophically broken", e.g. 5 tok/s) rather than a regression budget, and keep the env override you already have. A tight budget on shared-runner wall-time will page whoever's on call for noise.

## 2. `MIN_CPU_CUDA_AGREEMENT ≥ 70%` is too lenient to catch the bug it's for

This is the guard meant to catch a CUDA kernel that computes something different from CPU — exactly the class of #298 and #375 (grouped-int4 decoded as int2: "one correct token, then garbage"). But those bugs disagree *far* more than 30%, and 70% would **pass a kernel that's wrong on 3 of every 10 tokens**. On greedy argmax, CPU and CUDA should agree ~100% — floating-point accumulation order only flips the argmax on near-ties, which is rare. A real threshold is ≥95%, or better: **token-identical on a fixed short greedy prompt** (`TEMP=0`, same as the #163/#294 invariant). 70% isn't a correctness gate, it's a "roughly the same model" gate, and it would have greenlit the exact kernels we just fixed.

## The dossier is exactly right, don't change it

The opt-in `efficiency.py` that turns on every flag and prints "WHAT / WHEN / IS IT INEFFICIENT / HOW TO IMPROVE" per signal, never failing CI — that's the tool I've been hand-rolling all week. No notes; ship it. The auto-skip-when-no-CUDA is also correct so `make test` stays green on CPU-only.

Happy to review the PR properly once those two thresholds are addressed. If you'd rather, I can push the work-unit assertions (PROFILE-sums-to-100, experts/token bounds) as a small addition on top — they're the timing-free half and I've got the tiny oracle set up here to test them. Genuinely glad you're building this; it's the missing floor under every perf claim in the repo.

issue.title: docs/WINDOWS.md vs docs/windows.md case collision — Windows/macOS checkouts can never be clean, rebase refuses to run
issue.body:
## Summary

`dev` tracks **both** `docs/WINDOWS.md` and `docs/windows.md` — two different files (100 vs 90 lines, different content) whose names collide on any case-insensitive filesystem. On native **Windows and macOS** — the platforms half the recent contributions target (#198, #296, #329, #364…) — the repo **can never have a clean working tree**: both index entries map to one on-disk file, so whichever gets checked out last overwrites the other, and `git status` permanently shows one as modified.

```
$ git ls-tree upstream/dev --name-only docs/ | grep -i windows
docs/WINDOWS.md
docs/windows.md
```

## Where they came from

- `docs/windows.md` (lowercase, "# Windows 11 (native, no WSL)") — added in `6429123` (README restructure).
- `docs/WINDOWS.md` (uppercase, "# Windows 11 native install — a complete walkthrough (no WSL)") — added in `69284fa` via **#329**.

Presumably #329 didn't see the lowercase file on the author's case-insensitive machine — which is exactly the failure mode this bug creates.

## Impact (hit today, on real work)

On a Windows checkout of `dev`:
- `git status` always reports ` M docs/windows.md` (or `WINDOWS.md`) — a phantom that survives `git checkout -f`.
- **`git rebase` refuses to run** ("You have unstaged changes"), and `--autostash` loops: the stash applies and the tree is instantly dirty again. I had to `git update-index --assume-unchanged` both paths just to rebase #370's branch.
- Any workflow that assumes a clean tree (bisect, stash-based tooling, `gh pr checkout` guards) trips over it.

## Fix

Pick one canonical file, fold in anything unique from the other, `git rm` the loser (a plain commit on a case-sensitive Linux box can do it; on Windows use `git rm --cached`). Given #329's walkthrough is the newer, fuller doc, keeping **`docs/WINDOWS.md`** and deleting the lowercase one seems right — unless the lowercase build-focused content should merge in first.

Optionally, a one-line CI guard prevents regressions:
```sh
test -z "$(git ls-files | sort -f | uniq -di)" || { echo "case-colliding paths"; exit 1; }
```

Happy to PR the deletion+merge if you tell me which content survives.

comment.body: On `dev` now — a fresh checkout should be clean and `git rebase` should run again. If you can, `git pull` (or a clean clone) on your Windows/macOS box and confirm `git status` is clean with no phantom `docs/WINDOWS.md`/`docs/windows.md` conflict. There's a single `docs/windows.md` now, with both the walkthrough and the reference folded in. A green report from a case-insensitive filesystem — the environment none of the Linux boxes here reproduce — is what confirms it end to end.

issue.title: sampling: a single NaN/+Inf logit makes dist_build emit token 0 forever, silently (default path)
issue.body:
## Summary

On the **default** serve/chat sampling path (`TEMP>0`, `0<NUCLEUS<1`), a single **NaN or +Inf** logit makes `dist_build()` produce an all-NaN distribution, after which the sampler silently emits **token 0 forever** — an unbroken run of one token, with **no error printed**. A diagnosable upstream numerical fault becomes silent corruption.

## Mechanism (traced against `glm.c`)

`dist_build()` softmax:
```c
float mx=lo[0]; for(int i=1;i<V;i++) if(lo[i]>mx) mx=lo[i];
double s=0; ...
for(int i=0;i<V;i++){ g_pbuf[i]=expf((lo[i]-mx)*invt); s+=g_pbuf[i]; }
for(int i=0;i<V;i++) g_pbuf[i]/=(float)s;
```
- A `NaN` logit → `g_pbuf[that]=expf(NaN)=NaN` → `s=NaN` → every `g_pbuf[i]/=(float)s` is `NaN`.
- (`+Inf` → `mx=+Inf` → `expf(Inf-Inf)=NaN` → same collapse.)
- Nucleus keep-loop `cum+=g_pbuf[...]; if(cum>=g_nuc)` never triggers (`NaN>=x` is false) → `keep=V`, `s2=NaN`, still all-NaN.
- `dist_sample`: `cum>=u` never true → fallback `for(i=V-1..0) if(g_pbuf[i]>0) return i` is false for all (`NaN>0` false) → **returns 0**.
- Greedy path is equally blind: `argmax_v` seeds `bv=lo[0]` and `lo[i]>NaN` is always false, so a `NaN` at index 0 pins the argmax to **0**.

issue.title: convert_fp8_to_int4.py: --mtp is silently ignored with --indir and overwrites the finished container with a full int8 conversion
issue.body:
### Commit or version

`dev` @ d5327e2 (also present in earlier trees)

### What happens

`convert_fp8_to_int4.py --indir <local_fp8> --outdir <dir> --mtp` does **not** convert the MTP head. The local-directory branch of `main()` never passes `keep_mtp` (or `keep_idx`) to `convert_shard()`:

```python
if a.indir:    # conversione locale (test)
    ...
    convert_shard(sp, out, a.n_layers, a.ebits, a.io_bits, a.xbits,
                  group_size=a.group_size, bits_map=bits_map)   # <- no keep_mtp
    save_file(out, os.path.join(a.outdir, f"out-{i:05d}.safetensors"))
```

So with `--mtp` the flags silently degrade to: `keep_mtp=False` (MTP layer skipped, i.e. the opposite of what was asked) plus `ebits=8` (the `--mtp` default). The result is a **full int8 re-conversion of the whole model**, written to `out-00000..out-00140.safetensors` — the *same filenames* as a normal conversion in that outdir.

### Why this is worse than a no-op

The natural workflow is: convert the main model into an outdir, then run the documented second pass `--mtp` into the **same outdir** (that's where `out-mtp-*.safetensors` belongs). On the download path that's exactly right. On the `--indir` path, the second pass instead **overwrites the finished container shard by shard** with int8 per-row data, at ~40 s/shard, printing nothing that hints anything is wrong.

We hit this today while building the public g64 container for #298/#326: the `--mtp` pass destroyed 137 of 141 freshly converted fmt=4 shards before we caught it (the FP8 source was still on disk, so it cost ~5 h of re-conversion, not the container).

### Suggested fix

Either wire the flags through the local branch and write the correct names:

```python
if a.indir:
    shards = sorted(glob.glob(os.path.join(a.indir, "*.safetensors")))
    from safetensors.numpy import save_file
    prefix = "out-mtp-" if a.mtp else "out-idx-" if a.indexer else "out-"
    for i, sp in enumerate(shards):
        out = {}
        convert_shard(sp, out, a.n_layers, a.ebits, a.io_bits, a.xbits,
                      keep_mtp=a.mtp, keep_idx=a.indexer,
                      group_size=a.group_size, bits_map=bits_map)
        if out:
            save_file(out, os.path.join(a.outdir, f"{prefix}{i:05d}.safetensors"))
```

(with empty-shard skipping so `--mtp` emits only the shards that contain layer `n_layers`, and ideally a merge into a single `out-mtp-00000.safetensors` to match the download path) — or, as a minimal guard, refuse the combination outright:

```python
if a.indir and (a.mtp or a.indexer):
    sys.exit("--mtp/--indexer are not supported with --indir yet")
```

Happy to turn either into a PR if you tell me which shape you prefer.

### Environment

Windows 11 native, the #306 box (Core Ultra 9 285K / 128 GB / RTX 5080). Reproduces with any local FP8 checkpoint directory.

comment.body:
Just to close the loop with a concrete try-it: the fix is on `dev` now — please pull and confirm on your setup before you rebuild the public g64 container.

```bash
git pull            # dev
# main pass into an outdir, then the head pass into the SAME outdir:
python3 c/tools/convert_fp8_to_int4.py --indir <fp8> --outdir <dir> --group-size 64
python3 c/tools/convert_fp8_to_int4.py --indir <fp8> --outdir <dir> --group-size 64 --mtp
```
The second (`--mtp`) pass now writes `out-mtp-NNNNN.safetensors` and leaves the `out-NNNNN` container shards **untouched** — verify with an `md5sum` of one main shard before and after the mtp pass; it should be identical. Under the old code it would have been overwritten. Thanks again for catching this before it cost anyone else 5 hours.

issue.title: telemetry: hits conflates pin-tier and ecache hits — split them so eviction-policy changes are measurable
issue.body:
The expert lookup counts a hit identically whether it was served by the pinned tier or by the LRU ecache: both Phase C branches bump the same `m->hits` (`c/glm.c:3057` pin, `:3059` ecache, on dev), the struct has only the one lumped field (`:195`), and the PROF harness reports the lumped value only.

That makes every cache-policy question unanswerable from logs. The pin tier is checked first and — with a warm usage profile — absorbs most hot experts, so the ecache may be serving anywhere from ~0% to a large share of hits, and nobody can tell which. Concretely blocked by this:

- **#223's cap-threshold question** ("at what cap does cloxcache start to win?") — a flat A/B can mean "the pin tier absorbed everything" or "genuine floor effect", and the counter can't distinguish them.
- **Prefill-burst pollution**: prefill and decode share one `moe()` path and one ecache; between phases the counters reset (`:4633`) but the cache contents don't — whether decode actually pays for prefill pollution is currently unmeasurable.
- **PILOT_REAL eviction pressure** (the reason `PILOT_K` defaults 8→6 under real loads) — same problem.

Proposal (implemented on a branch): two counters `hit_pin` / `hit_ecache` bumped at the two lookup branches, surfaced in the periodic serve stats line (`hit N% (lru M%)` — pin share = N−M), the REPLAY summary (`expert hit N% (pin X% + lru Y%)`), and the `[PROF]` report (`hit N% (X pin + Y lru / Z load)`). The serve-mux `PROF` protocol line is untouched (openai_server.py parses it positionally). Zero cost — two increments on paths that already increment `m->hits`.

Branch: https://github.com/KingIcyCreamProjects/colibri/tree/telemetry/pin-vs-ecache-hits — happy to open the PR.

comment.body:
Fixed on `dev` — please pull and give it a run. Your diagnosis was exact, right down to the two branches (`glm.c:3135` pin, `:3137` ecache) bumping the same `m->hits`.

Two counters `hit_pin`/`hit_ecache` are now bumped at those two lookup branches (the existing `m->hits++` stays, so all existing math is untouched), snapshotted in `ProfBase` and reset alongside `hits`. Surfaced in the human-readable summaries, exactly the three surfaces you asked for:

- **decode summary**: `expert hit rate 31.3% (pin 22.1% + lru 9.2%)`
- **`[PROF]`**: `hit N% (X pin + Y lru / Z load)`
- **tiny/validation**: `hit rate 88.1% (0 pin + 74 lru / 10 miss)`

The serve-mux `STAT` protocol line is left untouched (openai_server.py parses it positionally), as you flagged.

The invariant `hit_pin + hit_ecache == hits` holds by construction and I verified it numerically on the tiny oracle: `0 pin + 74 lru = 74 hits`, 88.1% — with no PIN, all hits are lru and pin is 0, exactly as expected.

**How to try it:**
```bash
git pull            # dev
make -C c glm
PIN=auto PIN_GB=4 ./coli run "..." --model <model>   # warm pin -> pin share > 0
```
and watch the `pin X% + lru Y%` split in the decode summary. With `PIN` off it's all lru; warm it and the pin tier's share appears.

This directly unblocks the thing you called out: #223's cap-threshold A/B is now readable — "the pin tier absorbed everything" vs "genuine floor" are finally distinguishable. If the split reads wrong on your setup (pin + lru not summing to the total), reopen and I'm on it — but the construction makes that hard. Thanks for the precise report and the ready-to-use proposal.

issue.title: Kimi K3 (2.8T MoE, 16-of-896 routed experts) — candidate for the streaming architecture?
issue.body:
Moonshot released Kimi K3 today (Jul 16) — 2.8T total params, MoE with 896 experts per layer and 16 active, 1M context, Modified MIT. Full weights are announced for ~Jul 27.

Some napkin math on why it looks like a natural colibri target:

- **Sparsity is even more favorable than GLM-5.2**: 16-of-896 (~1.8%) routed activation vs GLM-5.2's 8-of-256. If per-expert size is in the same ballpark, per-token expert reads — the thing this engine is bound by — shouldn't grow with total model size.
- **The constraint moves from RAM to disk**: int4 would be roughly ~1.4 TB, so it stops fitting internal laptop storage — but a fast external TB4/TB5 NVMe seems plausible given the access pattern is read-only random reads.
- **The non-trivial part**: K3 introduces Kimi Delta Attention and attention residuals, so this isn't a config change — attention/KV would need engine work. (Whether it ships an MTP-style head I haven't seen confirmed yet.)

Is K3 support something you'd consider for the roadmap once weights land? Happy to help test on Apple Silicon (M4 Max — same box as #308).

comment.body:
Yes — **Kimi K3 is on the roadmap for colibrì the moment the weights are public** (announced ~Jul 27). Confirming it here so it's on the record.

Your napkin math is the right lens, and the sparsity point is the decisive one for this engine: 16-of-896 (~1.8% active) is *more* favorable than GLM-5.2's 8-of-256, and since colibrì is bound by **per-token expert reads, not total model size**, a bigger-but-sparser model isn't automatically worse here — that's exactly the regime this project exists for. If per-expert size is in GLM's ballpark, K3's per-token disk traffic could be comparable despite 2.8T total.

Two things we already see the same way:
- **The constraint moves RAM → disk**: int4 ≈ 1.4 TB stops fitting internal laptop storage, so K3 realistically needs a fast external NVMe (TB4/TB5). The access pattern (read-only random reads of experts) is exactly what an external NVMe handles well, and it's the same direction the streaming design already assumes.
- **The real work is the architecture, not a config**: Kimi Delta Attention + attention residuals mean the attention/KV path needs genuine engine work, not a parameter file. That's the part that will take time, and it's why "on the roadmap" is honest rather than "trivial."

The way this lands well (learned from the Inkling/DeepSeek engine PRs, #312/#165): a new architecture reaches `main` when it can be **verified** — a token-exact oracle against `transformers` on a tiny random-init model (like `make_glm_oracle.py` builds `glm_tiny/`), so the attention math is provably correct before anyone streams 1.4 TB. Your M4 Max offer is genuinely useful there — Apple Silicon is a platform the maintainer can't test on, and a second machine that can run the oracle is worth a lot.

So: parked as a real roadmap item, not a maybe. When the weights drop, the first milestone is the tiny oracle for Kimi Delta Attention; everything else follows from that being green. Thanks for the early, well-reasoned writeup — ping this issue when the weights are up.
