issue.title: sampling: a single NaN/+Inf logit makes dist_build emit token 0 forever, silently (default path)
issue.body:
## Summary

On the **default** serve/chat sampling path (`TEMP>0`, `0<NUCLEUS<1`), a single **NaN or +Inf** logit makes `dist_build()` produce an all-NaN distribution, after which the sampler silently emits **token 0 forever** — an unbroken run of one token, with **no error printed**. A diagnosable upstream numerical fault becomes silent corruption.

## Mechanism (traced against `glm.c`)

`dist_build()` softmax:
```c
float mx=lo[0]; for(int i=1;i<V;i++) if(lo[i]>mx) mx=lo[i];
double s=0; ...
for(int i=0;i<V;i++){ g_pbuf[i]=expf((lo[i]-mx)*invt); s+=g_pbuf[i]; }
for(int i=0;i<V;i++) g_pbuf[i]/=(float)s;
```
- A `NaN` logit → `g_pbuf[that]=expf(NaN)=NaN` → `s=NaN` → every `g_pbuf[i]/=(float)s` is `NaN`.
- (`+Inf` → `mx=+Inf` → `expf(Inf-Inf)=NaN` → same collapse.)
- Nucleus keep-loop `cum+=g_pbuf[...]; if(cum>=g_nuc)` never triggers (`NaN>=x` is false) → `keep=V`, `s2=NaN`, still all-NaN.
- `dist_sample`: `cum>=u` never true → fallback `for(i=V-1..0) if(g_pbuf[i]>0) return i` is false for all (`NaN>0` false) → **returns 0**.
- Greedy path is equally blind: `argmax_v` seeds `bv=lo[0]` and `lo[i]>NaN` is always false, so a `NaN` at index 0 pins the argmax to **0**.

There is no `isfinite`/`isnan` guard anywhere between the logits and the sampler.

## Reachability

The precondition is the *default*, not an edge case (`TEMP` auto≈0.7, `NUCLEUS` default 0.90). A non-finite logit is plausible in this NVMe-streaming engine: a garbage/short-loaded expert tile or an fp overflow in `matmul_qt` at a low-RAM eviction boundary. Both `dist_build` call sites are affected (the sampler and the speculative-verify path).

## Impact

Not a fault *originator* — it needs an upstream NaN/+Inf. It's a fault **amplifier**: it turns a catchable numeric fault into silent, unbroken token-0 output. Medium severity: it defeats loud failure on the default path.

## Fix (ready)

Minimal, O(1)/free on the happy path, on `fix/logit-nan-guard` (off `dev`) on my fork:
- `argmax_v`: skip NaN (`x==x`) and seed from `-inf` → returns the max finite/+Inf entry, not a NaN-pinned 0.
- `dist_build`: after the softmax sum, if `s` is non-finite or `<=0`, collapse `g_pbuf` to a one-hot over the finite argmax and warn once — **degrade + diagnose, never silently corrupt**.
- `test_logit_nan` (wired into `TEST_BINS`): fails on stock `dev`, passes with the guard.

Heads-up: since **#354** rewrites `dist_build`'s internals (heap partial-select), the `dist_build` half of this guard should be re-placed on top of that once it lands — happy to open the PR against whichever base you prefer, or fold it into the #354 work. @woolcoxm

comment.body:
Fixed on `dev`. You were exactly right, mechanism and all: a single NaN or +Inf logit poisoned `dist_build` — +Inf became `mx`, the softmax sum went NaN, every probability went NaN, and `dist_sample`'s fallback (`if(g_pbuf[i]>0)`) is false for NaN at every index, so it returned token 0, silently, forever.

`dist_build` now computes the max over finite logits only, gives a non-finite logit probability 0, and when the distribution is unusable (no finite logit, or a non-finite/zero sum) it collapses to a delta on the finite argmax and **warns once on stderr** — degraded output with a visible cause, never a silent stream of zeros. The argmax uses the index found during the max pass, so it's correct even when `lo[0]` itself is the NaN (where the plain `argmax_v` would wrongly return 0).

Shipped with `tests/test_sample_nan.c`: healthy logits still sample correctly; NaN/+Inf injected at position 0, the middle, and the last index all pick the finite argmax; an all-non-finite vocab leaves no NaN in the buffer and doesn't crash. Thanks for the clean bisect — a silent corruption on the default path is the worst kind, and you nailed it. Closing.

issue.title: docs/WINDOWS.md vs docs/windows.md case collision — Windows/macOS checkouts can never be clean, rebase refuses to run
issue.body:
## Summary

`dev` tracks **both** `docs/WINDOWS.md` and `docs/windows.md` — two different files (100 vs 90 lines, different content) whose names collide on any case-insensitive filesystem. On native **Windows and macOS** — the platforms half the recent contributions target (#198, #296, #329, #364…) — the repo **can never have a clean working tree**: both index entries map to one on-disk file, so whichever gets checked out last overwrites the other, and `git status` permanently shows one as modified.

```
$ git ls-tree upstream/dev --name-only docs/ | grep -i windows
docs/WINDOWS.md
docs/windows.md
```

## Where they came from

- `docs/windows.md` (lowercase, "# Windows 11 (native, no WSL)") — added in `6429123` (README restructure).
- `docs/WINDOWS.md` (uppercase, "# Windows 11 native install — a complete walkthrough (no WSL)") — added in `69284fa` via **#329**.

Presumably #329 didn't see the lowercase file on the author's case-insensitive machine — which is exactly the failure mode this bug creates.

## Impact (hit today, on real work)

On a Windows checkout of `dev`:
- `git status` always reports ` M docs/windows.md` (or `WINDOWS.md`) — a phantom that survives `git checkout -f`.
- **`git rebase` refuses to run** ("You have unstaged changes"), and `--autostash` loops: the stash applies and the tree is instantly dirty again. I had to `git update-index --assume-unchanged` both paths just to rebase #370's branch.
- Any workflow that assumes a clean tree (bisect, stash-based tooling, `gh pr checkout` guards) trips over it.

## Fix

Pick one canonical file, fold in anything unique from the other, `git rm` the loser (a plain commit on a case-sensitive Linux box can do it; on Windows use `git rm --cached`). Given #329's walkthrough is the newer, fuller doc, keeping **`docs/WINDOWS.md`** and deleting the lowercase one seems right — unless the lowercase build-focused content should merge in first.

Optionally, a one-line CI guard prevents regressions:
```sh
test -z "$(git ls-files | sort -f | uniq -di)" || { echo "case-colliding paths"; exit 1; }
```

Happy to PR the deletion+merge if you tell me which content survives.

comment.body:
Fixed on `dev`. `docs/WINDOWS.md` and `docs/windows.md` differed only in case — a genuine collision that makes every case-insensitive checkout (default Windows/macOS) permanently dirty and blocks rebase, exactly as you described.

Everything links to the lowercase `docs/windows.md` (README, README.zh-TW, docs/cuda.md), so that's now the single canonical file, with both contents folded in: the #329 install walkthrough followed by the build-flags/warmup reference. `docs/WINDOWS.md` is gone; no links changed. CI's windows and macos jobs (the ones a collision would trip) are green on the merge.

issue.title: sampling: DSA top-keep full-qsorts nk scores per layer per token — partial-select is ~10-24x cheaper (measured)
issue.body:
## What

`attention_rows()` finds the top-`index_topk` (2048 for GLM-5.2) context keys to attend to by **full-qsorting all `nk` attention scores** on every call, then reading the threshold off `tmp[keep-1]`. `nk` is the current context length, so this is `O(nk · log nk)` **per layer × per token** in decode, for every conversation that has grown past `index_topk`.

```c
// glm.c:2589-2596  (DSA lightning indexer, per-position selection)
float *tmp=falloc(nk); memcpy(tmp,isc,nk*sizeof(float));
qsort(tmp,nk,sizeof(float),cmp_fdesc);          // <-- full O(nk log nk) sort
float thr=tmp[keep-1];
int *dst=m->dsa_sel+(int64_t)s*dtopk, nd=0;
for(int t=0;t<nk && nd<keep;t++) if(isc[t]>thr) dst[nd++]=t;   // tie-break by position
for(int t=0;t<nk && nd<keep;t++) if(isc[t]==thr) dst[nd++]=t;
```

The sort exists only to find the `keep`-th largest score (the **threshold**); the two scans that follow pick the kept positions **in position order**, which is what fixes tie-ordering. So the qsort is doing far more work than the contract needs: it fully orders `nk` elements to extract one pivot.

This is the same class of inefficiency as **#335** (sampling `dist_build` full-vocab qsort → heap partial-select), but on the attention side, and it scales with **context length** rather than vocab size — so the cost *grows over the lifetime of a conversation*.

## Measured

Standalone microbenchmark (compiled `-O3 -march=native`, median of 2000 reps, `keep = index_topk = 2048`), old qsort vs a quickselect partial-select that partitions so the `keep` largest land in `[0,keep)`:

| nk (ctx len) | qsort (old) | partial-select (new) | speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2049 | 111,000 ns | 5,200 ns | **21×** |
| 4096 | 260,700 ns | 18,200 ns | 14× |
| 8192 | 616,900 ns | 60,300 ns | 10× |
| 16384 | 1,340,000 ns | 68,500 ns | 20× |
| 32768 | 2,840,700 ns | 289,200 ns | 10× |
| 65536 | 6,374,300 ns | 260,700 ns | **24×** |

At a 32K-token conversation × 78 layers, the qsort path alone costs roughly **2.8 ms × 78 ≈ 220 ms per generated token** — time spent sorting an array whose only purpose is to yield a single threshold value. The partial-select drops that to single-digit ms total.

The gap widens with context because qsort is `O(nk · log nk)` while quickselect is `O(nk)` average. DSA only activates past `index_topk` (2048), so this bites precisely on long conversations — the regime where decode latency matters most to the user.

## Correctness contract

This one is actually **cleaner than #335**. The qsort is **unstable** and only the threshold value (`tmp[keep-1]`) is read from the sorted array; the actual kept-position *set* is determined entirely by the two position-order scans:

```c
for(int t=0;t<nk && nd<keep;t++) if(isc[t]>thr) dst[nd++]=t;   // strictly-above first
for(int t=0;t<nk && nd<keep;t++) if(isc[t]==thr) dst[nd++]=t;  // then ties, in position order
```

So **any** algorithm that produces the correct threshold value (the `keep`-th largest score, with ties resolved by the position scan) yields a **bit-identical** `dst[]` to the current code. The quickselect pivot *is* by definition the `keep`-th largest element, so the new threshold equals the old `tmp[keep-1]` exactly. No multiset relaxation needed (unlike #335, which could only promise multiset equality because the heap was unstable and changed accumulation order).

The only thing to handle is the `keep`-th value when there are ties at the boundary — but the position-order `==thr` scan already makes the kept set deterministic given `thr`, so as long as the threshold value is identical, the kept set is identical.

## Proposed fix

Replace the `qsort` with a `partial_select_desc()` quickselect (Hoare partition, desc) that moves the `keep` largest into `tmp[0..keep)` in `O(nk)` average, then take `thr = min(tmp[0..keep))`:

```c
static void partial_select_desc(float *a, int n, int k);
...
float *tmp=falloc(nk); memcpy(tmp,isc,nk*sizeof(float));
partial_select_desc(tmp,nk,keep);            /* O(nk) avg, not O(nk log nk) */
float thr=tmp[0]; for(int t=1;t<keep;t++) if(tmp[t]<thr) thr=tmp[t];
```

The two position-order scans are unchanged → identical tie handling → identical `dst[]`.

`cmp_fdesc` becomes unused after this; I'll leave the definition in place (the existing `-Wno-unused-function` keeps the build clean, and the test/bench reuse the same comparator convention).

## What I checked and did NOT file (so it isn't re-investigated)

During this review I measured three other suspected hotspots and found them **already optimal at GLM-5.2 scale** — recording here so nobody else spends the time:

- **MoE top-K selection** (`glm.c:2913-2917`) is `O(K·E)` repeated linear-max-with-skip. At E=256, K=8 that's 2048 comparisons, and a branch-predictable linear scan measures **~3 µs** vs **~10.5 µs** for a full argsort. **Changing it would be a regression.** (The repeated scan wins because K·E ≪ E·log E at these sizes.)
- **`softmax`** (`glm.c:1292`) is 3 passes (max, exp+sum, divide). Precomputing `1/s` (2-pass) measures **within 1%** — the compiler already hoists the loop-invariant reciprocal. Not worth touching.
- **`tok_encode`** (`tok.h:247`) looks `O(L²)` at first glance but is **`O(L·nsp·s)`** — the inner position loop breaks on the first special-token hit and the outer loop breaks when none is found. Linear in text length.

## Secondary (not in the primary PR)

The `CACHE_ROUTE=1` opt-in path (`glm.c:2834-2850`) does `O(Mwin²·E)` repeated linear-max-with-skip scans when building `rank_buf`. Non-default and experimental — worth a separate follow-up issue, not bundled with this hot-path fix.

## Deliverables

- [ ] `partial_select_desc()` in `glm.c`, replacing the qsort in the DSA path
- [ ] `c/tests/test_dsa_select.c` — element-wise identical kept-set across shapes/sizes (stronger than test_topp's multiset check)
- [ ] `c/tests/bench_dsa_select.c` — old qsort vs new partial-select, the table above reproduced in-repo
- [ ] Makefile wiring (test in `TEST_BINS`, bench on-demand like `bench_topp`)

/cc reference: #335 (the sampling-side precedent this mirrors)

issue.title: sampling: dist_build full-qsorts all 151K vocab entries per sampled token — partial top-p select is ~7x cheaper (measured)
issue.body:
`dist_build()` (`c/glm.c:4193` on dev) sorts the **entire vocabulary** (`qsort(g_pidx, V, ...)` at `:4201`) on every sampled step whenever `0 < NUCLEUS < 1` — which is the serve default (temp resolves to 0.7, NUCLEUS=0.90), so it runs once per emitted token in normal serving, and additionally per draft position under rejection sampling (`:4343`).

Measured on a Ryzen 9950X3D (gcc 16.1, V=151936, GLM-5.2): the qsort with its indirect comparator costs **5.6–8.0 ms/call**; a partial selection (extract descending probabilities until cumulative mass ≥ `g_nuc` — typically a few hundred tokens at p=0.9) costs **~0.78 ms** → **~5 ms/token** of pure serial critical-path CPU saved. On disk-bound configs that's under the noise floor, but it's free tail latency on every config, it compounds with drafting, and it grows into a real fraction as I/O keeps improving.

**The correctness invariant** for any implementation: `dist_sample()` (`:4210`) iterates all V entries of `g_pbuf` directly and relies on `dist_build` having **zeroed the truncated tail** (`:4204`). A partial select must still zero every out-of-head entry, or the sampling distribution silently changes — no crash, just wrong sampling.

Implementation + invariant test on a branch: https://github.com/KingIcyCreamProjects/colibri/tree/perf/topp-partial-select

- max-heap over the index array: heapify O(V), pop descending until cumulative mass reaches `g_nuc`; winners land in `g_pidx[n..V-1]`, the remaining heap prefix is exactly the tail to zero
- `s2` is accumulated in descending order like the old code, so the renormalized head is **bit-identical** (boundary ties were unspecified under the unstable qsort and stay so)
- `tests/test_topp.c` drives the real `dist_build` (the `test_stops.c` include pattern) against an independent reference of the old algorithm: 6 sizes × 4 p values × 5 shapes including all-equal and plateau tie cases plus the `nuc>=1` guard-off path; asserts keep count, bitwise head equality on tie-free shapes, multiset equality on ties, exact-zero tails, and renormalization

Happy to open the PR.

issue.title: cuda: batch ragged attention across independent streams
issue.body:
## Summary

- add a CUDA MLA attention path for batches whose rows belong to independent KV sequences
- raise the multiplex decode batch ceiling from 64 to 512 rows
- keep DSA and unsupported cases on the existing fallback path
- add a CUDA correctness test against per-sequence batch attention

## Validation

- `make -C c glm CUDA=0`
- CUDA build on RTX 5090 (sm_120)
- `tests/test_ragged_attention`: `ragged_relative_rms=0`

## Measured result

On the 6x RTX 5090 full-residency GLM-5.2 setup at B=256, the decode forward changed from 26.131 s to 14.344 s (1.82x), while the attention phase changed from 15.143 s to 3.303 s (4.58x). Disk reads remained zero.

comment.body:
Reviewed the CPU-safety and it's clean — every glm.c change is behind `COLI_CUDA`, it merges cleanly on current `dev`, and the CPU build is warning-free. Track record noted (#132/#232/#255 all landed solid), so I'm inclined toward this.

The one thing holding the merge: it's an **attention batching kernel** — correctness-sensitive — and I have no GPU here, so I can't run `test_ragged_attention.cu`. That test is exactly the right artifact; I just can't execute it. Could someone on GPU hardware (@woolcoxm / @mohamedmastouri2000-boop are both active on CUDA right now) post the `test_ragged_attention` result on real silicon, ideally with a token-identity check vs the non-batched path on a short prompt? Ragged batching across streams is the kind of thing where a boundary/masking mistake produces subtly-wrong-but-not-crashing output, so the token-exact A/B is the gate.

With a green test from hardware, this merges. It stacks naturally with #298's CUDA work, so coordinating the two is worth it.

issue.title: feat(vk): opt-in Vulkan iGPU backend for quantized expert matmul
issue.body:
## What

An **opt-in** Vulkan compute backend (`backend_vulkan.c/.h` + one int8/int4 GEMV shader) that runs resident/pinned expert matmuls on a local GPU. Gated by `VK=1` at build time and `COLI_VULKAN=1` at runtime.

## The default build is untouched

Every `glm.c` hook is inside `#ifdef COLI_VULKAN`. Without the flag, **not a single VK symbol is referenced** — verified by `nm` (pure-C binary: 0 `coli_vk_*` symbols; VK build: 7) and by preprocessing `main` vs this branch under pure-C flags (the only non-`#ifdef` delta is one intentional fail-fast guard if a `VK_*` env var is set on a CPU-only binary). `make`/CUDA builds are byte-identical.

The int4/int8 shader is numerically verified against the CPU path on real hardware (Radeon 8060S / RADV, Strix Halo): max rel err ≤ 1.7e-4.

## Honest performance note

Targeted at the Strix Halo iGPU. Under **unified memory** the GPU and CPU share one ~150–175 GB/s bus and S=1 decode is memory-bound, so this is break-even for resident experts and net-negative once experts are streamed — it only pays off on a host whose GPU has its own high-bandwidth VRAM. Shipped off-by-default accordingly; the toggles document this.

## Toggles

- `VK_EXPERT_GB` — caps the iGPU-pinned expert tier.
- `VK_STREAM` (**default OFF**) — also routes streamed/cached experts through the shader; keeps both the CPU slab and a VK copy (~2× cache RAM), so opt-in for discrete-VRAM hosts only.
- `VK_DENSE` — extend to dense tensors. `VK_SPV` — override the shader path.
- Only int8 (fmt 1) / int4 (fmt 2) run on the GPU; fmt 0/3 and any omp-parallel context fall back to CPU, and a failed upload sets `vk_failed` to degrade gracefully.

## Robustness

Bounded fence wait (10s → disable VK/CPU-fallback on device-loss, never hangs); atomic backend counters (frees run under `#pragma omp parallel`) with symmetric weights+scales accounting; SPIR-V size validation; device selection prefers a real GPU over a software adapter; tensor free skips `vkDestroy` on a lost device (no GPU-side UAF).

## Build

`make VK=1` (needs `libvulkan` + `glslc`).

Closing this, and I want to be straight about why rather than let it rot: it's 245 commits behind, and — the deciding factor — there's no way to verify it. Nobody on the maintainer side has an iGPU Vulkan path, and CI can only prove it compiles, which for a quantized-matmul backend isn't enough to ship responsibly.

That's the same bar every backend faces here now (it's why the AMD/HIP and ARM kernels are in the same holding pattern): a compute backend lands when it can prove correctness, not just build. issue.title: feat: implement I/O-aware RAM fallback router and native string-indexed memory
issue.body:
This PR introduces two major performance features:

1. **I/O-Aware RAM Router:** Detects if the model is running on a slow HDD and actively tracks disk latency during the generation loop. If the disk starts bottlenecking, the router falls back to picking experts exclusively from the RAM-resident pool to maintain steady generation speeds.

2. **Native Local Memory:** Implemented a lightweight MemoryManager in the API server that logs conversation turns to a local text file and uses fast literal string matching to pull relevant historical context into the system prompt, entirely bypassing the need for heavy RAG databases.

issue.title: Windows/CUDA port: thermal gate, storage probe, correct dashboard stats
issue.body:
## Summary

Windows + CUDA portability pass so the engine and web dashboard run correctly on Windows with an NVIDIA GPU, plus a couple of small correctness fixes. Every change is guarded by `#ifdef COLI_CUDA` and cross-platform `#ifdef`s, so **Linux/CPU behavior is unchanged**.

## Changes

### Engine (`c/glm.c`)
- **NVML thermal heartbeat + `thermal_gate()`** — a background thread samples GPU temperature (dynamically loaded `nvml.dll` / `libnvidia-ml.so`) and applies a proportional per-forward pause to bleed heat under sustained load. `SAFE_MODE=0` disables it; if NVML is absent the engine proceeds normally.
- **Storage bus probe** — `probe_storage_drive()` / `storage_bus_is_slow()` check free space and disk bus type via Win32 `IOCTL_STORAGE_QUERY_PROPERTY` and POSIX `statvfs`. `FORCE_SLOW_DRIVE=1` bypasses.
- **Win32 `hwinfo_emit` branch** — cores/RAM/CPU brand via `GetSystemInfo` / `GlobalMemoryStatusEx` / `__get_cpuid`, so the dashboard works without `/proc`.

### Dashboard correctness
- Report **RAM and VRAM in binary GiB** so 128 GiB / 16 GiB read as `128` / `16` instead of decimal `137` / `17` — matches the spec-sheet convention users expect.
- Show the **real GPU model** via NVML `nvmlDeviceGetName` (e.g. `NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 Ti SUPER`) instead of a generic label. The web panel now displays the model name and labels the logical-processor count as **threads**.

### Server (`c/openai_server.py`)
- `COLI_EBITS` / `COLI_DBITS` env vars to select quant bit-width (needed for the 4-bit config on 16 GB GPUs). Absent → engine defaults (8/8), i.e. upstream behavior preserved.

## Testing
- Built on Windows with MinGW gcc + nvcc/MSVC (CUDA 12.9), `coli_cuda.dll` unchanged.
- Ran the full GLM-5.2 INT4 checkpoint end to end (`COLI_CUDA=1 CUDA_DENSE=1 CUDA_EXPERT_GB=6 RAM_GB=110`, 4-bit) with the web UI; `/health` reports `ram_total_gb 127.7`, `vram_total_gb 16.0`, `gpu "NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 Ti SUPER"`, coherent generation, thermal + storage probes firing.

issue.title: aarch64: add runtime i8mm expert kernel
issue.body:
## Summary

Add a runtime-dispatched AArch64 i8mm expert kernel for the existing int8-activation IDOT path.

- compile `SMMLA` only inside per-function i8mm targets, preserving the baseline AArch64 binary
- detect i8mm at runtime with `hw.optional.arm.FEAT_I8MM` on macOS and `AT_HWCAP2/HWCAP2_I8MM` on Linux
- compute a 2-output × 2-activation tile so all four `SMMLA` lanes do useful work
- use four independent accumulators to hide matrix-dot latency
- support both int8 and packed-int4 weights, arbitrary tails, odd `S`, and odd output counts
- keep the measured-slower int4 `S=1, I<6144` case on the existing NEON path
- add `I8MM=0` for same-binary A/B measurements

This keeps the dependency-free default CPU path and file formats unchanged.

## Why

The warm profile in #136 is matmul-bound on AArch64, while the current NEON IDOT path uses SDOT but not the available i8mm/SMMLA instructions. A direct instruction substitution was slower; the useful unit is a 2×2 tile with activation-pair packing and multiple independent accumulators.

## Validation

Apple M2 Pro (12 cores, 32 GB), macOS arm64, Apple Clang 21.0.0. The benchmark was single-threaded because local libomp headers were unavailable. It uses the real GLM-5.2 expert shapes, one untimed call per path, 21 timed A/B pairs with alternating order, and reports medians.

```text
$ make -C c OMPDIR= i8mm-bench
format shape              S   NEON-ms SMMLA-ms speedup  dispatch
i8   gate/up            S=1    0.867    0.526   1.648x  SMMLA
i8   down               S=1    0.652    0.529   1.233x  SMMLA
i8   gate/up            S=2    1.737    0.542   3.205x  SMMLA
i8   down               S=2    1.423    0.556   2.559x  SMMLA
i8   gate/up            S=4    3.445    1.015   3.394x  SMMLA
i8   down               S=4    2.638    0.986   2.675x  SMMLA
i4   gate/up            S=1    0.863    0.853   1.012x  SMMLA
i4   down               S=1    0.656    0.653   1.005x  NEON guard
i4   gate/up            S=2    1.725    0.850   2.029x  SMMLA
i4   down               S=2    1.305    0.853   1.530x  SMMLA
i4   gate/up            S=4    3.493    1.730   2.019x  SMMLA
i4   down               S=4    2.658    1.703   1.561x  SMMLA
```

Checks run:

- `make check OMPDIR=` — clean CPU build, C suite, and 62 Python tests pass with zero compiler warnings
- `./c/tests/test_idot` — exact against scalar references for int8/int4 tiles, random full-range weights, `-128` edges, tails, and `S=1..3` / odd-output dispatch
- x86_64 compile-only checks for `glm.c`, `test_idot.c`, and `bench_i8mm.c` — clean compile-out of the AArch64 path
- `git diff --check`

## Still needed before ready

I do not have the full model snapshot locally. Please run the token-exact oracle (32/32 TF + 20/20 greedy) and `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1 make -C c i8mm-bench` on an i8mm Linux machine/GB10 or Grace. `I8MM=0` provides the same-binary NEON control.

Tracks the aarch64 i8mm frontier item in discussion #209.

issue.title: fix(omp): park idle workers under PASSIVE policy (was spinning all cores at idle) #341
issue.body:
## Summary

Fixes #341 — \`glm\` stays at ~3000% CPU after the model finishes its answer.

## Root cause

The hot-thread tuning (added in #77) seeds \`OMP_WAIT_POLICY=active\` so libgomp's worker team stays hot across the back-to-back per-expert matmul regions. But **\`active\` keeps the team spinning in userspace forever between regions**: once a forward pass spins the team up, every worker core keeps busy-waiting even while the engine sits blocked in \`getline()\` waiting for the next prompt. On a many-core host that is the sustained ~3000% CPU the reporter sees at idle.

The original comment claimed \`GOMP_SPINCOUNT=200000\` would \"spin briefly, then yield\" — but that only holds under the **passive** policy. Under \`active\`, the spincount does not actually park the threads during long idle gaps.

This was independently corroborated by \`docs/METAL-M5MAX-PERF-REPORT.md\`, which already diagnosed the active-spin as harmful on Apple Silicon and worked around it with \`COLI_NO_OMP_TUNE\`. This PR generalizes that fix to the CPU path.

## The fix

Switch the wait policy to \`passive\` while keeping \`GOMP_SPINCOUNT=200000\`:

- **Between back-to-back matmul regions** (microseconds apart) workers are still inside their 200k-iteration spin window → stay hot → the #77 perf win is preserved (matmul 66.9s → 20.9s).
- **During the multi-second \`getline()\` idle**, workers exhaust the spin window in microseconds, then park on a futex → **0% CPU**.

## Changes

- \`c/glm.c\` — \`active\` → \`passive\` in the self-reexec tuning block, with corrected rationale comments
- \`c/coli\` — same fix in the win32 path of \`env_for\`
- \`c/tests/test_env_defaults.py\` — assertion updated \`active\` → \`passive\`
- \`docs/ENVIRONMENT.md\` — updated the \`COLI_NO_OMP_TUNE\` description

Metal/CUDA builds were already excluded from the tuning block (\`!getenv(\"COLI_METAL\") && !getenv(\"COLI_CUDA\")\`), so they are unaffected.

## Verification

- \`glm.c\` compiles clean (no new warnings).
- All 11 buildable C tests pass; \`test_env_defaults\` (4 tests) passes with the updated assertion.
- \`test_stops\` fails to build on Windows, but I confirmed it fails **identically on pristine \`origin/dev\`** — it is a pre-existing \`mkdtemp\` portability issue unrelated to this change.

I could not reproduce the 3000% figure locally (my CPU does not exhibit the same spin behavior), so the reporter's confirmation on their FreeBSD host would be the definitive test.

Closes #341.

comment.body:
Coordination note — `dev` already carries a fix for #341 (`d5327e2`): `KMP_BLOCKTIME=200` added alongside the existing `active`+`GOMP_SPINCOUNT` tuning, in both the glm.c re-exec and the coli launcher env. So the LLVM-libomp idle-spin (the actual #341 failure) is already resolved on `dev`, and I've since **reproduced it and verified the fix** empirically — posted the table on #341: libgomp `active`+spincount idles at 0%, libomp `active` at 1097% (11/12 cores), libomp + `KMP_BLOCKTIME=200` back to 0%.

Where your PR differs is one substantive change: **`OMP_WAIT_POLICY` active → passive**. That's the part I don't want to merge unverified, for a specific reason: my reproduction shows libgomp under `active`+`GOMP_SPINCOUNT` *already* idles at 0% — the spin cap works. So the passive switch isn't needed to fix the idle burn, but it *does* change the hot-path behavior during generation (passive parks the team differently between the tiny back-to-back expert matmuls), and the original `active` choice came with a measured number (#77: matmul 66.9s → 20.9s). Flipping it back could give some of that up, and I can't perf-test it here without a full model run.

So: the idle-burn half of your PR is already on `dev` and confirmed. If you believe `passive`+spincount preserves the generation-time perf that `active` bought, the thing that would let it land is a decode benchmark on the real model — same prompt, `active` vs `passive`, `experts loaded/token` and matmul-time from the PROFILE line — showing the 20.9s figure survives. If it does, passive is arguably cleaner and I'll take it. If it regresses, we keep `active`+`KMP_BLOCKTIME`, which already solves #341.

No rush — #341 is fixed either way, so this is an optimization-correctness question, not a bug. Thanks for digging into the same root cause; your writeup of the libgomp-forever-spin mechanism is exactly right.

issue.title: [Bug]: Nix flake fails to run tests, doesn't correctly package engine and server
issue.body:
### Commit or version

72d3d37231e922a6fa9afca16e08fa45842d5eb4

### Environment

NixOS, 256GB RAM, No GPU, GCC, NVMe

### Reproduction steps

Attempt to build the Nix flake with `nix build github:JustVugg/colibri` and observe:
```
$ nix build github:JustVugg/colibri
error:
       … while updating the lock file of flake 'github:JustVugg/colibri/72d3d37231e922a6fa9afca16e08fa45842d5eb4?narHash=sha256-tbbmqBsBi1FJUOjLrHm/DD/1e1VkLAiraGjBxra8Bnk%3D'

       error: cannot write modified lock file of flake 'github:JustVugg/colibri' (use '--no-write-lock-file' to ignore)
```
This happens because this repo is missing a `flake.lock`.  Then, attempt to fix this with `--no-write-lock-file` and observe the next error:
```
$ nix build github:JustVugg/colibri --no-write-lock-file
[snip]
       > python3 tools/run_tests.py tests/test_json tests/test_st tests/test_tier tests/test_grammar tests/test_schema_gbnf tests/test_decode_batch tests/test_idot tests/test_kv_alloc tests/test_i4_acc512 tests/test_compat_direct tests/test_uring
       > make: python3: No such file or directory
       > make: *** [Makefile:313: test-c] Error 127
```
This happens because the flake attempts to run tests in `checkPhase`, which needs Python, but Python isn't added to `nativeBuildInputs`, so it' s not available at build time.  To work around this, we can try to disable `checkPhase`:
```
$ nix build --impure --expr '(builtins.getFlake "github:JustVugg/colibri").packages.x86_64-linux.default.overrideAttrs { doCheck = false; }'
```
This finally builds the package, though without running tests.  Then, let's try to run the server:
```
$ coli serve --model ssd/glm52_i4
engine is not built. Run: coli build
```
The engine has been built and is contained in the Nix package, but it doesn't seem to be found by default for some reason.  Let's explicitly set `COLI_ENGINE` to the engine from the package, then (your store path may vary):
```
$ COLI_ENGINE=/nix/store/yf0gn1jv86zwfhdw7gq38rxmqf2424r5-colibri-1.0/bin/glm coli serve --model ssd/glm52_i4
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/nix/store/yf0gn1jv86zwfhdw7gq38rxmqf2424r5-colibri-1.0/share/colibri/coli", line 761, in <module>
    main()
    ~~~~^^
  File "/nix/store/yf0gn1jv86zwfhdw7gq38rxmqf2424r5-colibri-1.0/share/colibri/coli", line 756, in main
    if handler: sys.exit(handler(a) or 0)
                         ~~~~~~~^^^
  File "/nix/store/yf0gn1jv86zwfhdw7gq38rxmqf2424r5-colibri-1.0/share/colibri/coli", line 624, in cmd_serve
    from openai_server import serve
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'openai_server'
```
Oh no!  Looks like `openai_server.py` isn't packaged correctly, so that module fails to load.  `coli chat` works, though, if we set `COLI_ENGINE`.

### Expected behavior

The Nix flake builds a package containing a working colibri setup where the included engine is found by default and the server works.

### Actual behavior and logs

```shell
Included in reproduction steps above.
```

issue.title: convert_olmoe.py: --ebits is parsed but never used — every conversion silently emits int8, blocking the int4-on-OLMoE measurement
issue.body:
**What:** `tools/convert_olmoe.py` accepts `--ebits` ("Expert quant bits (4 or 8, default 4)") but the flag is never used — `quantize_row()` unconditionally emits int8 + f32 row scales, and `args.ebits` is not referenced after parsing. Every OLMoE conversion is int8 regardless of what the user asks for.

**Why it matters (per the #108 discussion):** the int8-vs-bf16 ppl A/B measured int8 experts as free (Δ ≈ 0.5% NLL, within noise — [#108 comment](https://github.com/JustVugg/colibri/issues/108#issuecomment-4993870843)). But the GLM container is **int4**, so the measurement that would actually bound the GLM quantization cost — int4-on-OLMoE, small enough to A/B both sides — is silently blocked: anyone who thinks they've produced an int4 OLMoE has been benchmarking int8. As bokiko noted there, nobody has actually measured int4 cost on OLMoE.

**Engine side:** `olmoe.c`'s runtime `bits` argument doesn't cover this either — for pre-quantized containers (dtype I8) `load_expert_w()` takes the raw-read fast path and the bits parameter only applies to bf16-container conversions at load.

**Offer:** happy to wire `--ebits` (int4 pack + scale layout mirroring the GLM container's grouped int4 would make the A/B maximally representative) and run the int4-vs-int8-vs-bf16 ppl A/B on the same fixed-token harness as the #108 datapoint. If a particular int4 layout is preferred (row-wise like current int8, vs 128-group like GLM), say the word and I'll match it.

— Peter Groom, Dawn Field Institute

issue.title: resource_plan: fix --auto-tier pinning decode to one core (#325)
issue.body:
Fixes #325.

## Problem

`--auto-tier` pinned decode to a single CPU core. The reporter (Rocky Linux 9, 512 GB RAM) observed that only one core was used with `--auto-tier`, and removing it "returned to normal." Root cause: `physical_cpu_count()` silently returned `1`, and that value flowed through `build_plan` → `environment_for_plan` → `OMP_NUM_THREADS`.

Two failure modes:

1. **The `lscpu` parse counted logical threads, not physical cores.** `lscpu -p=core,socket` *prepends the CPU column* (always first in parseable output — per the util-linux source), so the actual output is `CPU,Core,Socket`. The old set comprehension built `(CPU,core,socket)` tuples unique per *logical* thread — the SMT-doubled count the surrounding comments explicitly warn against.
2. **Any probe failure fell through to `os.cpu_count() or 1`.** If `lscpu` was absent, exited non-zero, timed out, or emitted a `-` for an offline core/socket (`int("-")` → `ValueError`, discarding the whole parse), control hit `os.cpu_count() or 1`. On a cgroup'd / `taskset`-pinned / constrained shell, `os.cpu_count()` can be `1` (or `None`) → exactly the single-core behavior reported. The `max(1, int(...))` clamp in `build_plan` then silently masked it.

### Why removing `--auto-tier` worked

On Linux the launcher sets OMP vars **only on Windows** (`env_for()`, `if sys.platform == "win32"`). Without `--auto-tier`, `OMP_NUM_THREADS` stays unset on Linux, so libgomp defaults to all cores — confirming the regression is isolated to the `--auto-tier` env-export path.

## Fix (`c/resource_plan.py`)

- Parse `lscpu -p=CPU,Core,Socket` and **dedupe on `(core, socket)`** → true physical cores.
- **Skip offline fields** (`-`) instead of letting one discards the whole parse.
- Replace the silent `os.cpu_count() or 1` fallback with `os.cpu_count()` (logical) **+ an explicit stderr warning**; the genuine "nothing detected" case still returns `1` but warns. Bad counts are now visible, never silent.
- Hardened the win32 branch: declared `argtypes`/`restype` on `GetLogicalProcessorInformationEx` (an undeclared 64-bit WinAPI silently returns the wrong type) + warning fallbacks.
- Replaced the silent `max(1, int())` clamp in `build_plan` with `_resolve_physical_cores()` that clamps to 1 *with a warning*.

## Testing

**Full Python suite: 77/77 passing** (`make test-python`).

**Core count — no longer 1 on Linux** (10 mocked `lscpu` topologies spanning the reporter's server class + the exact failure modes):
```
[OK] 2-socket x 32-core x 2-SMT  -> 64   (was 64 before too, but counted logical)
[OK] 2-socket x 16-core x 2-SMT  -> 32
[OK] 1-socket x 64-core x 2-SMT  -> 64
[OK] 4-socket x 24-core x 2-SMT  -> 96
[OK] one offline core field '-'  -> 3    (was: ValueError -> fallback -> wrong)
[OK] lscpu absent, os.cpu_count=128 -> 128  (was: silent 1 possible)
[OK] lscpu absent, os.cpu_count=1   -> 1   (now warns instead of silent)
```

**Resource allocation correct above 256 GB** — ran `build_plan` on the real `glm52_i4` (78 layers, 256 experts, 384 GB) at 256/512/800 GB + 1.5 TB. Both conservation invariants hold exactly in every case:
- `dense + runtime + cache <= budget` (never over-allocates RAM)
- `hot + warm + cold == expert_bytes` (no experts lost/duplicated between tiers)

At 512 GB (the reporter's box) and 800 GB (`--ram 800`): cap = 256/layer, 0 cold bytes, bottleneck = CPU compute. Correct.

**Regression tests added** (`c/tests/test_resource_plan.py`, 7 new):
- physical-vs-SMT dedup (multi-socket, single-socket, no-SMT)
- offline `-` field handling
- lscpu-missing fallback (uses logical + warns, never silent 1)
- zero-cores degenerate case
- end-to-end `build_plan` + `environment_for_plan` asserts `OMP_NUM_THREADS` = physical cores
- `test_plan_conserves_budget_and_experts_above_256gb` — locks in the >256 GB budget/expert conservation invariants at 256/512/800 GB

The pre-existing `OMP_NUM_THREADS` test passed `physical_cpus=24` explicitly and so never exercised the real probe — that is why this regressed.

Branch based off `dev`.

🤖 This PR was developed with ZCode.

---

> **Credit / reporter:** this bug was reported and iteratively diagnosed by **@liangstein** in #325 (Rocky Linux 9, 512 GB RAM host), who ran every iteration of the fix across several rebuilds and pinned down the `physical_cpu_count()` regression. The fix belongs to their report.
<!-- credit-liangstein-325 -->

comment.body:
Held back — the visibility improvements are great (every fallback should warn, and `_resolve_physical_cores` clamping visibly is exactly right), but the core lscpu change **regresses core detection on some machines**, and I caught it by running it here rather than reading it.

## The lscpu claim is inverted

The PR rests on:
> `lscpu -p` prepends the CPU column always, so `-p=core,socket` actually emits "CPU,Core,Socket" (3 columns).

That's backwards. `lscpu -p` (no list) prepends CPU; `lscpu -p=<list>` emits **exactly the requested columns, no CPU prefix**. Measured on this box (WSL2, 6c/12t):

```
$ lscpu -p           | head        →  0,0,0,0,,0,0,0,0     (CPU prepended, 9 cols)
$ lscpu -p=core,socket | head       →  0,0                 (2 cols, NO CPU)
```

## What that does to the new parser

The new code reads `fields[1]` (core) and `fields[2]` (socket), guarded by `if len(fields) < 3: continue`. On 2-column output **every line is skipped**, `cores` stays empty, and it falls through to `os.cpu_count()` — the logical count. Result on this machine:

| | physical cores returned |
|---|---|
| **old code** (`tuple(map(int, line.split(",")))` dedup) | **6** ✓ correct |
| **this PR** | **12** ✗ (logical — over-subscribes SMT) |

So on hardware where `-p=core,socket` gives two columns (the documented behavior), this turns correct 6-core detection into 12-thread over-subscription — the opposite of a fix.

## Why #325 saw "one core" anyway

The real bug in #325 is almost certainly the silent `max(1, int(...))` you correctly identified: on liangstein's box a probe failed and got masked to 1. **That half of your PR is the actual fix** — make probe failures visible and fall back to logical, not 1. Keep it.

## The minimal correction

Parse `-p=core,socket` as 2 columns (what it emits), and separately handle bare `-p` if you want CPU-prefix robustness — but don't assume the prefix. Something like:
```python
fields = [f for f in line.split(",")]
# -p=core,socket → [core, socket]; be tolerant of an optional leading CPU col
core, socket = (fields[-2], fields[-1])   # last two are always core,socket
```
using the **last two** fields rather than `[1],[2]` makes it correct whether or not a CPU column is present. Then the empty-set→logical fallback only fires on genuine failure.

Would you push that? The warning/visibility scaffolding is worth keeping — it's just the column indexing that needs to not depend on a prefix that isn't there. Happy to test the revised version here on the 2-column layout before it merges.

issue.title: feat(prefetch): async expert prefetcher v4 -- 94.3% cache hit rate, 2.8x speedup, zero RAM overhead
issue.body:
### Summary

This PR implements an asynchronous expert prefetcher (`PILOT` modes 0–3) for `olmoe.c`, significantly accelerating streaming inference directly from SSD storage.

By combining multi-layer lookahead prediction, stale request pruning, consolidated expert I/O, routing EMA (momentum logits), and persistent pre-warmed expert pinning, this achieves a **94.3% overall cache hit rate** (up from **62.4% baseline**) and a **2.8× speedup (1.26 → 3.54 tok/s)**, with **zero additional RAM overhead** (Peak RSS stays at 4.80 GB).

All code has been reviewed and hardened for thread safety, numerical stability, and memory safety before submission.

---

### Benchmark Results (OLMoE-1B-7B merged model, cache=32 experts/layer, bits=8)

| Version | Description | Cache Hit Rate | Speed | SSD reads/token | RAM (Peak RSS) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Baseline** | No Prefetch | 62.4% | 1.26 tok/s | 64 | 4.80 GB |
| **PILOT=1** | 1-layer lookahead | 79.3% | 1.83 tok/s | 35 | 4.80 GB |
| **Consolidated I/O** | 1 disk read/expert instead of 3 | 86.9% | 2.12 tok/s | 22 | 4.80 GB |
| **Prefetcher v3.0** | Deduplication, stale pruning, topk limit | 90.1% | 2.95 tok/s | 16 | 4.80 GB |
| **Prefetcher v3.1** | Routing EMA + dynamic pinning | 90.5% | 3.25 tok/s | 16 | 4.80 GB |
| **Prefetcher v3.2 (this PR)** | **Persistent pinning + pre-warmup (PILOT=3)** | **94.3%** 🚀 | **3.54 tok/s** 🚀 | **16** | **4.80 GB** |

*The hit rate during the decode phase alone reaches **95.2%** under the final configuration.*

---

### Core Enhancements

1. **Consolidated Expert I/O**
   - Merges `gate_proj`, `up_proj`, and `down_proj` weights into a single contiguous file (`merged_weight`) plus a `.qs` scale file per expert.
   - Reduces disk read syscalls from 3 per expert to 1, maximising sequential SSD throughput.

2. **Stale Request Pruning & Queue De-duplication**
   - Lock-free ring buffer (`pilot_q`) decouples prediction from loading.
   - On every new token step the queue is flushed so the background thread focuses on the current token's early layers immediately.

3. **Routing Momentum (EMA Logits)**
   - Tracks an exponential moving average (EMA) of routing logits across tokens (`SMOOTH` env var, default 0.3).
   - Stabilises prediction for recurring routing patterns and closes the early-token prediction gap.

4. **Persistent Hot Pinning & Pre-warmup**
   - Saves dynamic pinning masks to `hot_pinned.bin` in the snapshot folder after the warmup phase.
   - On subsequent runs the engine pre-loads these experts into the cache at startup, eliminating cold-start and prefill misses without extra RAM.

5. **Multi-layer Lookahead (PILOT=2/3)**
   - PILOT=2 dispatches prefetch at layer `i+2` (after MoE residual is available).
   - PILOT=3 adds an additional `i+3` speculative pass to hide even deeper I/O latency.

6. **Cross-platform Test Suite & Tooling**
   - Python helpers to download the model, convert to merged format, run determinism checks, and compare against the HF PyTorch reference.

---

### Implementation Quality

- **Thread safety**: all mutations of `is_queued` and the prefetch queue are guarded by `g_pilot_mx`. The worker thread is detached (`pthread_detach`) immediately after creation; thread creation failures exit cleanly.
- **Numerical stability**: candidate logits are shifted by `max_logit` before `expf()` to prevent overflow to `INF` in the softmax-style scoring loop.
- **Memory safety**: `malloc` return value is checked in `slot_ensure_allocated`; `hot_eids` array bounds are clamped to 256 to prevent stack overflow.
- **Wide prefetch**: `max_cand` is now correctly derived from `topk * g_wide` so the `WIDE` env var has the intended effect.
- **Dead code removed**: unused variables, the buggy `rebalance_cache()` function, and its associated `REBAL` env var have been removed entirely.

---

### Verification

- Compiled and tested on Windows/x86-64 with GCC (`-O2 -march=native -fopenmp`).
- **Determinism confirmed**: two consecutive runs produce identical token outputs (`Matching tokens: 12/12` vs HF oracle).
- **Peak RSS confirmed**: stays within the original 4.80 GB budget (no memory regression).
- **Hit rate confirmed**: 94.3% measured via `c/tools/test_olmoe_real.py`.

comment.body:
First: thank you — and not as a formality. This PR gets right, on its own, several things this repo learned the hard way over the past week:

- **The prefetcher changes residency, never values.** I read the EMA path carefully: the momentum buffer feeds only `pilot_prefetch`'s ranking, while the real routing keeps using the fresh gate logits. That invariant (a predictor must never alter what gets computed) is exactly what separates a safe optimization from a quality bug here, and you respected it without anyone asking.
- **Your headline metric is falsifiable.** `SSD reads/token: 64 → 10` is a counter that can be checked against reality, not a counter that measures its own intentions. After what happened with `~335 GB I/O saved` in #254/#303, this matters more than the speedup number itself.
- **You shipped an independent oracle** (transformers-based, not self-referential) alongside the feature. That's the standard we're trying to make normal.

Housekeeping: I've retargeted the PR to `dev` — everything lands there first now; `main` is release-only.

## The ask: would you run the same protocol on GLM-5.2?

Here's the honest reason, explained calmly rather than as an objection. Your OLMoE numbers are *credible in OLMoE's regime*: with `cache=32` of 64 experts per layer, the cache covers **50% of the expert pool**, so there's real headroom for prefetch to land in, and 62→94% hit is physically plausible. GLM-5.2 — the model this project is named for — lives in a different regime on the low-RAM machines colibrì targets:

- the cache holds **3-4 of 256 experts per layer (~1.5% coverage)**;
- GLM's routing is famously flat (see the measurements in #303 and the LOOKA numbers in #333: ~144 of 256 experts per layer carry 90% of traffic);
- during decode the disk is already saturated (~85-90% of wall time is expert-disk wait), so prefetch there can't create bandwidth — it can only keep the request queue deep and hide latency.

None of that says your design won't help GLM — multi-layer lookahead and the EMA predictor are exactly the kind of thing that pays on a latency-bound queue, and `glm.c` already has a PILOT ring your ideas could slot into. It says the *numbers* won't transfer: on GLM the win, if it's there, will look like +15-30% from queue depth, not 94% hit — and a **measured** +15-30% on the flagship would honestly be a bigger deal for this project than 2.8× on OLMoE.

If you take the GLM run, the protocol that will make it land without a second round:
- `TEMP=0 TOKENS=1`, one run `PILOT=0` and one with your best mode: **token streams must be identical** (your invariant makes this a formality, but it's the gate);
- cold-vs-cold (`AUTOPIN=0`, same or deleted `.coli_usage`, paste the `[RAM_GB=...] cap=` line) — warm/cold mixes have manufactured fake speedups here before;
- report `experts loaded/token` + hit rate as primary, wall clock as color (on VHDX-class disks we've measured the same binary varying 3× on wall time between runs).

And if OLMoE-only is the scope you want for this PR, that's completely fine too — say so and we'll review it as an OLMoE improvement on its own merits, with the oracle you already provided. Either way: genuinely good work, and welcome.

issue.title: cuda: COLI_GROUP_ASYNC=1 — overlap the CPU expert rows with the GPU groups at decode (opt-in, +6-8%)
issue.body:
## What

At S≤4 decode on a multi-GPU full-residency host, `moe()` runs two phases **serially**: the VRAM-resident experts' grouped GPU calls, and the CPU rows (RAM-tier + misses). Per-phase measurement on 6× RTX 5090 put them at roughly 50 ms + 50 ms per token — a textbook overlap opportunity.

This adds an **issue/take split** of `coli_cuda_expert_group` (launch on the device stream and return; sync at layer end) and a **two-pass moe block**: pass 1 collects the VRAM experts' groups and issues them async; the CPU loop then computes its rows *while the GPUs work*; a take phase collects and accumulates. Per-device failure recomputes on the CPU (`expert_host_ensure` reloads slabs released by `CUDA_RELEASE_HOST`); any issue failure drops the whole layer back to the existing sync path.

**Opt-in: `COLI_GROUP_ASYNC=1`, default off.**

## Measured (6× RTX 5090, full residency, warm segment 256→512 of a 512-token greedy run, `.coli_usage` snapshot-restored between arms)

| arm | warm decode | expert-matmul bucket |
|---|---|---|
| sync (today) | 4.04–4.09 tok/s | 53.0 s |
| overlap | **4.29–4.40 tok/s (+6–8%)** | **47.7–48.2 s (−9–10%)** |

Reproduced across two independent A/B rounds. The async-issue *without* the overlap is a wash (+1.5%) — the win is the concurrency, not the removed sync.

## The honest caveat (why default-off)

Greedy output under the overlap is **not byte-identical** to the sync path: a near-tie token flips early in the run (with usage state controlled, so it is *not* placement drift — some floating-point accumulation-order difference I have not yet pinned down; the kernels are byte-for-byte the sync path's small-batch kernels and the packing order mirrors the sync path's). Same class as the documented CPU-vs-GPU / kernel-family divergences (#100): every emitted token remains the argmax of a valid forward, but the stream forks. Under #294's standard the flag also implies it should stay off during speculation until verified — at S≤4 with drafts live, `SPEC_PIN` semantics take precedence.

Follow-ups I intend regardless of merge: (a) a position-aligned `ROUTE_TRACE` diff (the #163 method) to locate the first flipped layer and, if it's an ordering bug rather than inherent, fix it to byte-exactness; (b) profiling the remaining serial residue in the overlap window (take-phase device syncs, pass-1 packing).

comment.body: check cuda and i merge it!

issue.title: [Bug]: glm stays at 3000% CPU after the model finished the answer.
issue.body: ### Commit or version 72d3d37 ### Environment FreeBSD 15.1, NVMe 5.0, 64GB RAM. I asked a question, then pressed Ctrl-C, added corrections, then pressed Ctrl-C, repeted few more times, eventually the model finished the answer, but glm stays active. See the screenshot. ### Reproduction steps See above. ### Expected behavior glm's CPU use should go to zero once the model becomes inactive. ### Actual behavior and logs ```shell n/a ```

comment.body:
Update: **the mechanism is now reproduced and the fix verified empirically** — no longer a hypothesis.

We ran the exact idle scenario (one parallel region to spin up the team, then the process parks like the engine does on `stdin`) on a 12-core Linux box, three ways:

| runtime + policy | idle CPU after the answer |
|---|---|
| libgomp (GCC), `active` + `GOMP_SPINCOUNT=200000` — the shipped tuning | **0%** |
| **LLVM/Intel OpenMP runtime, `active`** (via `LD_PRELOAD=libiomp5.so`) | **1097%** — 11 of 12 cores spinning forever |
| same + **`KMP_BLOCKTIME=200`** — the fix on `dev` (`d5327e2`) | **0%** |

1097% on 12 cores is your 3000% scaled to core count. The LLVM runtime ignores every `GOMP_*` knob and, under `active`, spins the parked team indefinitely; `KMP_BLOCKTIME=200` is the knob it actually reads, and it sleeps the team while keeping it hot across back-to-back matmul regions.

So: pull `dev`, rebuild, and your idle CPU should drop to ~0 within a second of the answer finishing. The `ldd ./glm | grep -i omp` is now just confirmation of which runtime your build links — the fix covers the failing one either way.


comment.body:
Closing — the bug as filed is fixed, and your own last run proves it.

**What you reported**: the engine died mid-generation with no message at all — just `[engine terminated]` and a goodbye. That silence was the bug on our side: the kernel's OOM-killer uses SIGKILL, which leaves no trace, and the launcher never reaped the exit status.

**What your re-run shows** (thanks for pulling and recompiling):

```
[engine terminated: killed by SIGKILL]
nothing in the engine sends SIGKILL to itself: this is the kernel's OOM-killer...
```

The engine now names its killer, and the diagnosis is right there: your `free -m` shows **~5.5 GB actually available** of 64 (58 GB held by other software). GLM-5.2 needs ~11 GB for resident weights alone before any cache — with defaults it will be OOM-killed every time on that headroom, and now it *says so* instead of vanishing.

@bokiko's guidance is exactly right: size `--ram` to what's genuinely free (with your current workload that's not 50 — try freeing memory first, or `--ram` well under what `free -m` reports as *available*, leaving headroom for KV and activations). Current `dev` also refuses to start when the projected peak exceeds actually-available memory, so the crash moves from mid-generation to a clear message at startup.

If it ever dies again with a *different* cause line — anything other than the OOM SIGKILL — reopen and we're on it. Thanks for the clean before/after, it's the report that confirmed the whole diagnostic path works in the field.

comment.body:
One sharpening fact from the code archaeology, @yurivict:

Your build (`72d3d37`) **already had the libgomp spin cap** — `GOMP_SPINCOUNT=200000` shipped together with the original hot-team tuning in #77. On a GCC/libgomp build, your engine should have gone to sleep ~instantly at idle even before any fix. The fact that it spun at 3000% anyway points hard at **LLVM libomp** (which ignores every `GOMP_*` knob and, under `active`, spins forever) — so the `ldd ./glm | grep -i omp` from my previous comment is now not just a diagnostic, it's *the* deciding datum.

Status of fixes, so the thread is coordinated:
- `dev` already carries `KMP_BLOCKTIME=200` (`d5327e2`) — the knob libomp actually reads. If you're on libomp, pulling `dev` should already fix you.
- @woolcoxm's #349 proposes the same `KMP_BLOCKTIME` plus a switch to `passive`+explicit spin windows and extends the treatment to the Windows launcher env; it overlaps the dev fix and we're reconciling the two rather than stacking them.

Either way: `ldd` output + a retest on current `dev` (answer finishes → does CPU fall to ~0 within a second?) closes this.

comment.body:
@galmok — one more thing for your tests tomorrow, and it's the piece that ends the guessing.

Your `pread qs: Input/output error` was hiding the real Windows error: `compat_pread` collapsed **every** `ReadFile` failure into `EIO`, so storage faults, antivirus interference, memory pressure and a dozen other distinct causes all printed the same three words. As of `dev` (`211d448`), the failure line now carries the actual code:

```
pread qs: Input/output error (off 780592, 0/8192 bytes, WinErr=1450)
```

That one number is the diagnosis: `1450/8` = insufficient system resources (memory pressure), `32/33` = sharing/lock violation (AV or another process touching the shard), `21` = device not ready (the `T:` drive itself), `1117` = actual device I/O failure. Windows-compile is CI-certified.

So for tomorrow, ideally in this order:
1. Pull current `dev`, rebuild, rerun the joke prompts. If it dies again, **paste the `WinErr=` lines** — that settles in one shot what three of us have been guessing at.
2. @woolcoxm's #361 retry branch is worth testing too, as he asked — but note it's a *survivability* patch: knowing the error code first tells us whether those retries even target your failure.
3. His Event Viewer ask stands — disk/Ntfs/storahci entries at the crash timestamp would corroborate whichever story the WinErr tells.

And for the record from your last paste: the monastery joke was long, coherent, and repetition-free — **the original #307 symptom is gone on your box too.** What killed that run is a different animal, and now it will have a name.

comment.body:
Closing this as **fixed on `dev`**, with a verified reproduction chain behind it.

## What it was

`coli chat` silently resumed the previous conversation from `<model_dir>/.coli_kv` — even days later — and the launcher swallowed the engine's own announcement of it. The model then behaved "incoherently" while actually being perfectly coherent with a context nobody could see. On top of that, the engine only armed 3 stop tokens from the checkpoint's config, so a checkpoint with incomplete metadata could sail past the end of a turn printing control tokens as text.

## What's on `dev` (commit `d5327e2` or later)

- **Resume is announced before the chat starts** (`↺ resuming a saved conversation: N tokens, last written …`) with `:reset` / `KVSAVE=0` / delete-file guidance — no more invisible context.
- **Stop set hardened 3 → 18**: every token the tokenizer marks `special:true` now ends the turn, whatever the config says.
- **Engine deaths are named** (`[engine terminated: killed by SIGKILL]` + stderr tail).
- `coli chat` attaches to a running `coli serve` (model loads once), `coli stop` shuts everything down.

## Verification

@CorentinWicht reproduced the mechanism on his machine (a 31 MB `.coli_kv` from his earlier sessions was present), rebuilt `dev` at `7eb2393`, re-ran his repro: *"it worked perfectly well in my case, well done!"* — thank you for the fast, complete test report.

@galmok — if your case persists after pulling `dev`, please say so here and I'll reopen immediately: your symptom may also involve the second thread of this story, the **per-row int4 repetition attractor**, which is tracked separately (#225, and @woolcoxm/@mohamedmastouri2000-boop are close to publishing a public **g64 grouped container** that fixes it at the checkpoint level — #326/#298). Same if anyone else still hits an unstoppable answer on current `dev`: comment and it reopens, no ceremony.

Thanks to everyone on this thread — three reporters, one invisible mechanism, and the fix is better because each of you brought a different piece.

issue.title: [Bug]: glm stays at 3000% CPU after the model finished the answer.
issue.body: ### Commit or version 72d3d37 ### Environment FreeBSD 15.1, NVMe 5.0, 64GB RAM. I asked a question, then pressed Ctrl-C, added corrections, then pressed Ctrl-C, repeted few more times, eventually the model finished the answer, but glm stays active. See the screenshot. ### Reproduction steps See above. ### Expected behavior glm's CPU use should go to zero once the model becomes inactive. ### Actual behavior and logs ```shell n/a ```

comment.body:
Thanks for the report — there's a candidate fix on `dev` now, and one question that will confirm or kill the diagnosis.

## The mechanism we suspect

At startup the engine re-execs itself with OpenMP "hot team" tuning: `OMP_WAIT_POLICY=active`, so the worker threads stay spinning between the tiny per-expert matmul regions instead of sleeping (that's worth real tok/s during generation). On GCC's **libgomp** the spin is capped: `GOMP_SPINCOUNT=200000` → threads sleep almost immediately once idle, and on our Linux/gcc box the engine idles at ~0% after an answer.

**LLVM's libomp reads neither of those.** Under `active` it sets its own blocktime to *infinite* — so the moment the answer ends and the engine parks on `stdin`, the whole team spins forever: ~100% × nthreads, which is exactly your 3000%. FreeBSD's default toolchain neighborhood is clang/libomp, which is why we think you're the first to see it.

## The fix on `dev`

`KMP_BLOCKTIME=200` is now set alongside the GOMP knob (overwrite=0, so your own setting wins if you export one): the team stays hot for 200 ms after each parallel region — plenty to bridge consecutive expert matmuls — then sleeps. On libgomp builds the variable is ignored entirely, so this is a no-op for GCC users by construction.

## What would settle it

1. `ldd ./glm | grep -i omp` — is it `libomp` (LLVM) or `libgomp` (GCC)? If libomp, the mechanism fits your symptom exactly.
2. Pull `dev`, rebuild, repeat your repro (answer → wait): does CPU drop to ~0 within a second of the answer finishing?
3. If it does NOT: `top -H` (or `procstat -t $(pgrep glm)`) while it's burning, and paste which threads are hot — that tells us whether it's the OMP team at all or something else (your repro involved several Ctrl-C interrupts, and an interrupted-turn path leaving a worker spinning is our plan B to investigate).

One honest caveat: we could not reproduce on this machine (gcc/libgomp idles clean), so this ships as the mechanism-matching fix pending your confirmation — your box is the test bench for this one.

issue.title: [Bug]: Nix flake fails to run tests, doesn't correctly package engine and server
issue.body:
### Commit or version

72d3d37231e922a6fa9afca16e08fa45842d5eb4

### Environment

NixOS, 256GB RAM, No GPU, GCC, NVMe

### Reproduction steps

Attempt to build the Nix flake with `nix build github:JustVugg/colibri` and observe:
```
$ nix build github:JustVugg/colibri
error:
       … while updating the lock file of flake 'github:JustVugg/colibri/72d3d37231e922a6fa9afca16e08fa45842d5eb4?narHash=sha256-tbbmqBsBi1FJUOjLrHm/DD/1e1VkLAiraGjBxra8Bnk%3D'

       error: cannot write modified lock file of flake 'github:JustVugg/colibri' (use '--no-write-lock-file' to ignore)
```
This happens because this repo is missing a `flake.lock`.  Then, attempt to fix this with `--no-write-lock-file` and observe the next error:
```
$ nix build github:JustVugg/colibri --no-write-lock-file
[snip]
       > python3 tools/run_tests.py tests/test_json tests/test_st tests/test_tier tests/test_grammar tests/test_schema_gbnf tests/test_decode_batch tests/test_idot tests/test_kv_alloc tests/test_i4_acc512 tests/test_compat_direct tests/test_uring
       > make: python3: No such file or directory
       > make: *** [Makefile:313: test-c] Error 127
```
This happens because the flake attempts to run tests in `checkPhase`, which needs Python, but Python isn't added to `nativeBuildInputs`, so it' s not available at build time.  To work around this, we can try to disable `checkPhase`:
```
$ nix build --impure --expr '(builtins.getFlake "github:JustVugg/colibri").packages.x86_64-linux.default.overrideAttrs { doCheck = false; }'
```
This finally builds the package, though without running tests.  Then, let's try to run the server:
```
$ coli serve --model ssd/glm52_i4
engine is not built. Run: coli build
```
The engine has been built and is contained in the Nix package, but it doesn't seem to be found by default for some reason.  Let's explicitly set `COLI_ENGINE` to the engine from the package, then (your store path may vary):
```
$ COLI_ENGINE=/nix/store/yf0gn1jv86zwfhdw7gq38rxmqf2424r5-colibri-1.0/bin/glm coli serve --model ssd/glm52_i4
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/nix/store/yf0gn1jv86zwfhdw7gq38rxmqf2424r5-colibri-1.0/share/colibri/coli", line 761, in <module>
    main()
    ~~~~^^
  File "/nix/store/yf0gn1jv86zwfhdw7gq38rxmqf2424r5-colibri-1.0/share/colibri/coli", line 756, in main
    if handler: sys.exit(handler(a) or 0)
                         ~~~~~~~^^^
  File "/nix/store/yf0gn1jv86zwfhdw7gq38rxmqf2424r5-colibri-1.0/share/colibri/coli", line 624, in cmd_serve
    from openai_server import serve
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'openai_server'
```
Oh no!  Looks like `openai_server.py` isn't packaged correctly, so that module fails to load.  `coli chat` works, though, if we set `COLI_ENGINE`.

### Expected behavior

The Nix flake builds a package containing a working colibri setup where the included engine is found by default and the server works.

### Actual behavior and logs

```shell
Included in reproduction steps above.
```

comment.body:
Three of your four failure modes are fixed on `dev` — @woolcoxm's #348 is merged. Verified each claimed cause against the actual code before merging:

1. **`make: python3: No such file or directory`** — `checkPhase` runs `make test-c`, which shells out to `python3 tools/run_tests.py`; `python3` was missing from `nativeBuildInputs`. Added.
2. **Engine never found** — the old layout put the engine at `$out/bin/glm` and the `coli` script at `$out/share/colibri/`, but `coli` resolves its engine via `COLI_ENGINE` → beside-the-script → `libexec/colibri`, none of which matched. New self-contained `$out/lib/colibri/` layout + `COLI_ENGINE` set by the wrapper.
3. **`coli serve` import failure** — `openai_server.py` / `resource_plan.py` / `doctor.py` were never copied; now they sit beside the script with `PYTHONPATH` set.

**What is NOT fixed: the missing `flake.lock`** — your first error stands. Neither the PR author nor this box has Nix available to generate one honestly. Since you're on NixOS: would you send a trivial PR with the generated `flake.lock` (and, while you're in there, confirm `nix build` + `nix run .#coli` now work end-to-end on `dev`)? You're the only one in this thread who can actually validate the result on real Nix — a green report from you closes this issue.

One known nit we left for a follow-up: the wrapper uses `--set COLI_ENGINE`, which overrides a user-provided `COLI_ENGINE`; `--set-default` would respect it. If you touch the flake for the lock file anyway, feel free to fix that too.

comment.body:
Update — and a correction to my own earlier diagnosis. We kept digging, reproduced the bug in-house, and found that **at least part of this is not the model at all. It's us.** The fixes are on `dev` now and I'd like to ask you both to test it.

## What we found (reproduced locally, mechanism confirmed in code)

`coli chat` (SERVE mode) **persists the conversation KV to `<model_dir>/.coli_kv` and silently resumes it on the next chat** — even days later, even in a "fresh" session. Resume-without-re-prefill is a deliberate feature; the bug is the *silence*, twice over:

1. The engine does announce it (`[KV] resumed conversation from disk: N tokens`) — but the launcher's stderr drain races the message, so on a healthy start **the notice was never shown**. You only ever saw engine stderr when the engine *died*.
2. Resumed turns are appended with `first=0`, i.e. **without the `[gMASK]<sop>` prefix**.

Our reproduction: a chat suddenly answered in Italian and kept rewriting its own story ("here is the complete corrected version…"). It looked exactly like model degeneration. It wasn't: the engine was **being coherent with 670 tokens of an old Italian conversation that nobody could see.** After removing `.coli_kv`: same prompt, clean English, no rewinding.

This may not be your whole story — see below — but it produces precisely the reported *shape*: works fine for a while, then a later session/turn goes off the rails and never stops.

## What's on `dev` for you to test

- **Resume is announced client-side** before the chat starts: `↺ resuming a saved conversation: N tokens, last written 2d ago` + how to start clean (`:reset`, `KVSAVE=0`, or delete the file). No more invisible context.
- **Stop set hardened 3 → 18**: the engine now also stops on every token the *tokenizer* marks `special: true` (`<|user|>`, `<|assistant|>`, `<sop>`, …), whatever the checkpoint's config says. A converted checkpoint with incomplete `eos_token_id` can no longer print control tokens as text and sail past the end of a turn.
- **Engine deaths are named** (`[engine terminated: killed by SIGKILL]` + stderr tail) instead of the old silent `[engine terminated]`.
- Bonus: `coli chat` can now attach to a running `coli serve` (model loads once, stays warm), and `coli stop` shuts everything down — no more hunting processes.

## What I'd ask you to check

1. Does `<your_model_dir>/.coli_kv` exist? Size and date? (If yes — that's very likely your culprit, and just deleting it should change everything.)
2. Pull `dev`, rebuild (`make -C c glm`), rerun your repro. If it resumes, it will now *say so*.
3. Paste the `[stop] N stop tokens:` line from startup — on the per-row int4 checkpoint it should now say 18, not 3.

## What this does NOT close

@woolcoxm's observation stands: on his box the pure repetition disappears on the **gs64 (group-scaled) container** and exists on per-row int4 — so quantization remains a live suspect for the *attractor* itself, especially for within-session degeneration like @CorentinWicht's numbered-responses case. Two mechanisms can both be real: stale-KV explains the "coherent-with-invisible-context" cases; per-row scales may explain the tight loops. `TEMP=0.9 NUCLEUS=0.95` remains the sampling mitigation for the latter.

If the `.coli_kv` check comes back positive for either of you, this issue splits cleanly — and the half we own is already fixed. Thanks for the patience; the invisible half of this took us a while to see too.
